author: ByronBrown
text: Introduction: I have owned 3 bikes, a Yamaha 125 twin, a Kawasaki 175 trail and the incomparable Z1. I bought the newly arrived Z1 on impulse and never regretted it. A friend, Peter, (I recently helped him get his M/C licence), invited me to the showrooms. For the test drive we were bluntly told if you drop it you buy it so Peter gave me the nod. I had an immediate affinity with the big bike. I loved the turbine whine, reminded me of the jumbo jet take offs. Confident to command, comfortable to travel and later I discovered the awesome stopping power during an altercation with a drunk driver. The Z1 out stopped, by a large margin, a 4 wheel locked-up squealing large sedan travelling in the same direction. It was the first time I saw the front forks travel, yawning the frame into a we mean business stop. Back to the test drive and I chucked a very reluctant Peter off the bike to see the difference and saw none. Skinny Pete then had a go, we returned and the surprised vendor made 2 sales. News of my then top of the line bike purchase quickly got around. Now this soon happened twice within a few days. I was asked, by two different people, why 900cc? A 250 or maybe 300 tops would have stacks of power and on both occasions I felt like a gasping fish out of water, caught me off guard, didn´t make me feel good at all, shook my tree a bit. A few days later I realized the answer. Look directly at the guy with a big genuine smile and say "Have you ridden one?" and boy was I ready for number three. A great bike, possibly one of the greatest of its time in my limited view as I would be hard pressed to suggest improvements. On thing though, the exhaust system had started to rust along a weld seam after one year. Perhaps stainless steel next time? Thank you for the opportunity to contribute to this forum. Byron Brown.

Iam glad you are about to enjoy yours.  Good riding.

text: i bought mt z1 the week between christmas and new years 1974-1975.its color was blue,i had three to choose from 2 maroon and one blue..i traded a cb550 for the bike.the bikes price was $2750.00 brand new.it was incredibly fast.. i put denco cams,air pods and a kerker header on it, rejetted the carbs,i ran A/ss thats A super stock at the local dragstrip.it ran 11.90s at around 105 mph all day..had it painted all black with the chrome engine kit it was a stunning machine,should have kept it..sold it in 1980 for $1800.00.best bike i have ever owned and that counts 3 hondas and 2 harleys..this thing ate sportsters with ease..thanks for reading..

author: Cal
text: My issue....the high beam indicator lamp on the instrument cluster will become dimly lit with the headlight on low beam (at idle) and become brighter as the RPMs increase. The high/low switch works and will act as it should along with the high beam indicator and headlight. What´s really weird tho...as I ride with the low beam on, the H/B indicator lamp continually gets brighter until I use a turn signal, then it goes dim again. It´s like I´m building capacitance somewhere in the system? I have the US and European schematics and went as far as completely removing all wiring (again) and checked it with a DMM then rechecked it with everything mounted and powered up. I can´t find anything wrong? Anyone have any ideas? (I used LED replacements in the cluster but it does the same thing with OE bulbs so I´m at a loss) Thanks for any ideas you send my way!!! Ride Safe!

author: R.goon14
text: joe, you may have got a WP booklet with your bike which tells you everything about your forks and how to set them up. on the bottom of the forks they will be a screw. you turn them both clockwise until they stop clicking for the hardest setting. and for the average setting i think you turn them 14 clicks BACK. on the top of the forks there is to rebound adjusters and you should just have a fiddle with him. hope this helped. your best getting a WP booklet thing.

text: yeah mate, 1 down 4 up. neutral is a half click between 1st and 2nd. its not noticable when riding, just make sure you dont go finding neutral when going around corners, it doesnt help your corner speed much lol

text: Haven´t had that problem, ask your dealer for the factory update on the fuel injection program, if they know nothing about it put on some aftermarket cans and go for the full factory race tune.

text: How can i get the full factory tune? I have the cans, but I live in the U.S.,so there is nothing here for it. Dearler´s can´t help with a new F.I. map.

author: Drag Duke Oz
text: If they are a dedicated KTM dealer, they should be able to get the tune from there importer, keep trying, it´s there, if your local dealer can´t help, find a dealer who is willing to go that extra bit for his customer, ask if there chief mechanic gets the factory training, ´cause they should know about it.

author: kingmingus
text: Is the factory tune a new map or map & Ign. map both? Is it a download from a PC? Is it something that can be sent to me? Thanks

author: Drag Duke Oz
text: I think it is just a map and I´m pretty sure you can only get it from a dealer, it will definitly increase output and i will know in a few weeks once my system goes on and gets put on the dyno.

author: kingmingus
text: Please let me know how that works out for you. My local KTM dealer is trying to get some info. on the Factory Tune. They dont think they will be vary helpfull sence the 990 super duke has no support in the U.S. Anything you can find out about it would be helpfull,what tools (P.C.or some KTM tool) they use to do the upgrade,can I send my ECU in and have it done,etc. etc. Thanks-Lynn

author: Drag Duke Oz
text: Here they just plug in a lap top into the computer and download a new fuel map, maybe easier to try someone outside the U.S. Try these guys, they are my dealer and have been number 1 in Australia several times, actionmc@qldnet.com.au Attention Chris, they might be able to help.

author: kingmingus
text: Thanks I will do that. Let me know how your bike comes out after the race tune. K&N, Im told will have a filter for the (950) by the 1st of the year, same filter as the 990 S.D. My black S.D.has 9250 miles on it with nothing more than tires and oil changes and valve ADJ. Only wish I lived at sea leavel not 5000 Ft.I have been on it at sea leavel--WOW. I hope your dealer will help me with the Race Tune. Thanks - Lynn

author: Drag duke oz

text: Haven´t had that problem, ask your dealer for the factory update on the fuel injection program, if they know nothing about it put on some aftermarket cans and go for the full factory race tune.

text: How can i get the full factory tune? I have the cans, but I live in the U.S.,so there is nothing here for it. Dearler´s can´t help with a new F.I. map.

author: Drag Duke Oz
text: If they are a dedicated KTM dealer, they should be able to get the tune from there importer, keep trying, it´s there, if your local dealer can´t help, find a dealer who is willing to go that extra bit for his customer, ask if there chief mechanic gets the factory training, ´cause they should know about it.

author: kingmingus
text: Is the factory tune a new map or map & Ign. map both? Is it a download from a PC? Is it something that can be sent to me? Thanks

author: Drag Duke Oz
text: I think it is just a map and I´m pretty sure you can only get it from a dealer, it will definitly increase output and i will know in a few weeks once my system goes on and gets put on the dyno.

author: kingmingus
text: Please let me know how that works out for you. My local KTM dealer is trying to get some info. on the Factory Tune. They dont think they will be vary helpfull sence the 990 super duke has no support in the U.S. Anything you can find out about it would be helpfull,what tools (P.C.or some KTM tool) they use to do the upgrade,can I send my ECU in and have it done,etc. etc. Thanks-Lynn

author: Drag Duke Oz
text: Here they just plug in a lap top into the computer and download a new fuel map, maybe easier to try someone outside the U.S. Try these guys, they are my dealer and have been number 1 in Australia several times, actionmc@qldnet.com.au Attention Chris, they might be able to help.

author: kingmingus
text: Thanks I will do that. Let me know how your bike comes out after the race tune. K&N, Im told will have a filter for the (950) by the 1st of the year, same filter as the 990 S.D. My black S.D.has 9250 miles on it with nothing more than tires and oil changes and valve ADJ. Only wish I lived at sea leavel not 5000 Ft.I have been on it at sea leavel--WOW. I hope your dealer will help me with the Race Tune. Thanks - Lynn

After every running at the motocross for training, there is a click sound or pinking and it disturbs me so much and feel that there is something wrong with the engine. author: sfahringer
text: I own a 2004 KTM 300 exc. FMF Knarly Pipe, 5 rides on a top end rebuild. The bike was running great; I had taken a log to the pipe hwich bent it and caused there to be an exaust leak atthe cyl head; killed power and loaded up trying to get back to the truck. Fixed the pipe; went riding and everything was fine. Last 10 minutes of teh last ride; pipe springs failed, exaust leak again and loading up and power down - loading up. Now have new springs, pipe quiet; but hard to start, smoking a ton and loading up at idle. if i get on it and clear it out; power is up and runs like normal. If I putt, loading up and smoke, smoke smoke? Not Smelly- normal 2 stroke smell?? Help?

author: John Graham
text: The Super Mini Duke 390 Is ready for you! Are you ready for the Duke? I´m not some pro rider or anybody special. But I have owned more than over 100 motorcycles. This 2016 Duke 390 is one of the funniest bikes I´ve ever owned. Sits up strait and is plenty quick enough to make a pass and cruise down the freeway. Can not believe the power this bike has for its weight and engine size. A beginner would love this bike because you may be satisfied enough to put off upgrading to a bigger bike. Heck I can own any bike I want and this bike does it all. I purchased new for $3,995 what a deal. I feel like I stole this bike. So if you want lots of fun and unlimited good times, get one.

author: Tim H
text: I have a 2004 factory racing editon  KTM 450 Sx. Got it on a trade and saving to rebuild bottom end. In doing this, I´ve done countless research... Everyone has said that the 4speed, designed-for-track-transmission makes the bike too high-geared to trail ride or for in the woods. Also, there is no tps on this bike which I´ve heard makes throttle gentle throttle difficult unless twisting to wide open throttle. With that being said, there are only two tracks in a 100 mile radius of me and it WILL be tested in the woods somewhat lol. There is supposed to be a difficult, expensive method of adding 5th and 6th gear to the original trans but then the ratios would be out ta sack and you´d still probably have to replace 1-4 to match them. These bikes are designed, manufactured, and tested with only one thing in mind - TRACK SPEED. Needless to say I don´t think it will make the best kind of all around track/woods bike. However in around a month, mine will have basically a brand new engine top to bottom and regardless of where I ride it, it will face the gauntlet. ;) I plan on my buddy who has raced for almost 20 years to Lao it at the track as well as testing it myself on whatever kind of woods terrain I have the balls for lol so it will be slightly abused and then probably sold to fund a 250sx-f or 300 2stroke lol. I will let u know how she does. Hope this somewhat helps.

However, have heard a few problems (starter, suspension, etc). Does anyone have an ´05 525 EXC who can give positive and especially negative feedback? Please e-mail responses to scs2rescue@yahoo.com Thanks, Alan

author: Tony
text: Hi Alan, I have a 2005 525 EXC, has done 2500km, has been serviced every 500km (oil change and filters) + 1 valve clearance. Once the suspension has been sorted for a heavier rider (I am 100 kg) new springs for the front were needed. Apart from that it is a fantastic bike, lots of power, great handling and light weight. Just today I did the regular 500km service and much to my dismay on the sump magnet looks to be part of a gear tip. Not sure of the outcome yet but will know soon enough.

author: Hanomag940
text: Living in the Andes of Peru, between 9000 and 16000 feet altitude, I would like to know what is the autonomy of this bike with the standard fuel tank? Is this bike realy reliable (the only KTM workshop in Peru is in Lima, at 500 km of the place where I´m living).No overheating problems? Is this model agil in the small footpads?

author: Alan
text: Hanomag, I, too, am interested in this bike & I´m in search of information. Try these great sources of review: www.thumpertalk.com www.ktmtalk.com I also found this article below: "Actually the new 525 EXC that my wife gave me for Christmas is a great riding and running machine. My big disappointment is the starter that stripped gears on the second ride. Whenever the bike is shut off or stalls, the crank turns backwards somewhat and puts severe stress on the starting system all the way to the starter motor itself. I only know what causes the problem because I also had a 2001 520 EXC that did the same thing. I thought that four years later I would not need to worry about that problem. Anyone interested in buying one of these great bikes should be aware of the cost of the repairs involved." Wow, Andes of Peru????? Very interesting!  Were you born there? What´s it like? I´d appreciate you forwarding any information you find to my e-mail: scs2rescue@yahoo.com Alan

author: John DeRaspe
text: Hi!  I currently have an XR400R (98) and I ride a lot of tight trails with steep hills and plenty of rocks.  My 400 gets awful heavy throwing it around in this type of terrain.  In addition it is a bear to start in some situations.  I am preparing to extend my riding into more open and faster trails in addition to tight trails.  I am over 6´4" and 240 without riding gear.  I am 40 years old and am also wanting to get into some Enduro racing and scrambles for fun.  I have been riding dirt and street since I was 8.  I have just started getting serious about the KTM 525 EXC since it is lighter than even the 450 Enduros out there and packs a bigger punch which I sometimes need due to my size. My questions are...Is this bike a reliable choice?  I want to be able to set it up for me and ride reliably for some time without blowing expensive parts.  From what I have seen parts are just as cheap, if not cheaper, than the Japanese Bikes.  Is this true?  I don´t think any of these new 4 strokes could possibly be as reliable as my XR but are they even close.  Is it true this bike will handle better than my XR in the tight stuff since it weighs less?  I am convinced it would handle better on open trails than my XR due to it´s updated suspension? Any help from knowlegable people would be appreciated.  Thanks John

author: Alan
text: John, I just posted a similar message to yours.  I, too, am looking for info on the new 525 EXC.  Try using the following sites: www.thumpertalk.com www.ktmtalk.com Here´s an article I´ve found so far: "Actually the new 525 EXC that my wife gave me for Christmas is a great riding and running machine. My big disappointment is the starter that stripped gears on the second ride. Whenever the bike is shut off or stalls, the crank turns backwards somewhat and puts severe stress on the starting system all the way to the starter motor itself. I only know what causes the problem because I also had a 2001 520 EXC that did the same thing. I thought that four years later I would not need to worry about that problem. Anyone interested in buying one of these great bikes should be aware of the cost of the repairs involved." I´d appreciate you sending me any information you find to my e-mail address: scs2rescue@yahoo.com Alan

author: Tony
text: Hi Alan, I have a 2005 525 EXC, has done 2500km, has been serviced every 500km (oil change and filters) + 1 valve clearance. Once the suspension has been sorted for a heavier rider (I am 100 kg) new springs for the front were needed. Apart from that it is a fantastic bike, lots of power, great handling and light weight. Just today I did the regular 500km service and much to my dismay on the sump magnet looks to be part of a gear tip. Not sure of the outcome yet but will know soon enough.

author: Alan
text: Hanomag, I, too, am interested in this bike & I´m in search of information. Try these great sources of review: www.thumpertalk.com www.ktmtalk.com I also found this article below: "Actually the new 525 EXC that my wife gave me for Christmas is a great riding and running machine. My big disappointment is the starter that stripped gears on the second ride. Whenever the bike is shut off or stalls, the crank turns backwards somewhat and puts severe stress on the starting system all the way to the starter motor itself. I only know what causes the problem because I also had a 2001 520 EXC that did the same thing. I thought that four years later I would not need to worry about that problem. Anyone interested in buying one of these great bikes should be aware of the cost of the repairs involved." Wow, Andes of Peru????? Very interesting!  Were you born there? What´s it like? I´d appreciate you forwarding any information you find to my e-mail: scs2rescue@yahoo.com Alan

text: Hello: Just wanted to see what others think of this bike. I´ve had it for a couple years now and all in all its been a good bike. I do have some concerns about its power delivery. Not sure if anyone else is having the same problem but it doesnt possess the smooth delivery you would expect. I have rebuilt the top end 3 times now and just can´t get it run as smooth as I like. text: Just recently bought my second Moto Guzzi v7 850... I have a 1995 sport 1100 that I bought new I´m just restoring it.... have a great day

author: Jeff Saine
text: I own a 2021 V7 Special.  For me, its a near perfect middle distance touring and local bike.  Looks good, functions very well, reliable and economical.  I typically get 50+mpg, often close to 60.  I have Hepco-Becker C-bar side bags and with the bags its great for an over-nighter.  It does have things that are Guzzi unique, like a really clunky transmission.  It really CLUNKS into each successive gear but I like it.  Its quiet, has good road manners and is not as slow as some would have you think. I´ve had mine to 113 mph with a tail wind and I think it had more to give.  Downside:  Aprillia business practices.  They just don´t seem to understand the American customer.

author: Desmond C Dennis
text: The 2021 Moto Guzzi V7 Special is the most beautiful retro bike I have laid eyes on ...full stop. I am an 81 year old retired biker. This one I must have even if only to sit the bike in the shed and polish it frequently. Des aka Mick

author: Mike williams
text: Recently inherited a 1974 Guzzi 850 GT. The bike is in very good shape and mostly together. There are a lot of parts that came with this bike but I am sure I am missing some of the parts needed to put bike in running condition. Carburators are off and apart,Distributer is out and apart,rocker box oil lines are off and missing, and I need to know what type and size of battery it needs Bike was last together in 1985, was being ridden and ran good. Not sure why it was taken apart but I suspect it was for maintenance. My Father was the owner and had health problems which is why no further information is avaliable. also have a second Guzzi of similar year and size for sale. I am a mechanic by trade and any help or information on this(these) bikes would be appreciated. The first bike will be restored and ridden by Myself. Thanks in advance for your help,  Mike Williams.

author: trifstar
text: I´m a 60 year old Australian who´s been riding bikes for 44 years and raced for a number of years in the 250 production class (basically customer 250 GP bikes such as TZ Yamahas and RS Aprilias etc). Unlike many at my age, I still love riding sports bikes and own a MV Brutale 800 RR, and have owned everything from a CB200 Honda, ZX9R, ZX6R, GPX750 Kawasaki´s, and Moto Guzzi 850 Le Mans, Ducati Mille MHR and so on. I have ridden and honestly can´t see the attraction of cruisers. Maybe it´s different in a country like the U.S.A. with lots of straight long highways (though I´d still imagine I´d prefer to be on something like a KTM 1290 Duke Super GT in those circumstances). I voraciously consume technical and general information regarding motorcycles (maybe that´s the mechanical engineer side of me) and it is a source of general amusement to my friends that when discussing any bike I´ll rattle off the alphanumeric title of it and its´ weight etc. I´m honestly not in any way trying to show off it´s just these things seem to stick in my mind and there are so many similar models, it´s my way of ensuring we´re talking about the same bike. Apart from bikes, Moto Gp and bike racing in general, I also love Rugby (Union), Cricket, reading and film. So there you have it. You all know what there is to know about me!

author: trifstar
text: There is a glaring error in the technical facts regarding the MV Brutale 800. It is NOT air/oil cooled at all but very much liquid cooled as the performance of the engine indicates. It does have an oil cooler, as do many liquid cooled bikes but there is no air cooling in any way - other than air cooling the radiator! I hope this is updated quickly as, if people rely on this information alone, they might very well dismiss the bike from their consideration, much to their future detriment in terms of owning an absolutely brilliant bike - within its´ design parameters. It´s extremely unfair to be critical of this bike for its´ inability to tour or carry gear as MV didn´t build it with these functions in mind. That´s why they built a specific 800 model for touring purposes which is entirely different in engine and comfort characteristics.

author: ammar

Maybe it´s different in a country like the U.S.A. with lots of straight long highways (though I´d still imagine I´d prefer to be on something like a KTM 1290 Duke Super GT in those circumstances). I voraciously consume technical and general information regarding motorcycles (maybe that´s the mechanical engineer side of me) and it is a source of general amusement to my friends that when discussing any bike I´ll rattle off the alphanumeric title of it and its´ weight etc. I´m honestly not in any way trying to show off it´s just these things seem to stick in my mind and there are so many similar models, it´s my way of ensuring we´re talking about the same bike. Apart from bikes, Moto Gp and bike racing in general, I also love Rugby (Union), Cricket, reading and film. So there you have it. You all know what there is to know about me!

author: trifstar
text: There is a glaring error in the technical facts regarding the MV Brutale 800. It is NOT air/oil cooled at all but very much liquid cooled as the performance of the engine indicates. It does have an oil cooler, as do many liquid cooled bikes but there is no air cooling in any way - other than air cooling the radiator! I hope this is updated quickly as, if people rely on this information alone, they might very well dismiss the bike from their consideration, much to their future detriment in terms of owning an absolutely brilliant bike - within its´ design parameters. It´s extremely unfair to be critical of this bike for its´ inability to tour or carry gear as MV didn´t build it with these functions in mind. That´s why they built a specific 800 model for touring purposes which is entirely different in engine and comfort characteristics.

author: AJ
text: Greetings everybody. I´m the Chief of Police in a little northern Oklahoma town called Medford. I´ll be 64 next month with just a few years left to retirement. Back in my 20´s and early 30´s I rode a motorcycle a lot, riding 50 miles round-trip every day to and from work. I easily put 100,000 on one bike or another back then...mostly Kawasakis. When I sold my last one, the wife decided to keep me off of them. Fast forward to a year ago. The wife insisted on getting a little dog (yorkie) that I didn´t want. I decided since she got something she wanted that I didn´t, that I would get something I wanted that she didn´t...a bike. Started with a Kawasaki KLR650 enduro, but it was too tall for me. Found my way to an ´05 Suzuki Boulevard with about 6k miles on it, and love it....not quite as much as I loved my Kawasaki Z-1, or even my Kawasaki GPZ-550, but I do love going from a chain to a shaft drive. While I´ll putt around town bare headed in a t-shirt, when I leave town, I have jeans, boots, my mesh jacket, riding gloves, and full-face helmet...even tomorrow when I go to a family reunion in Ponca City, about 45 miles away...in 105 degree heat. That´s how I rode in my younger days, and I haven´t changed my ways.

author: john
text: I am in canada,bought a 2006 suzuki c50t new and rode it to argentina and back. SUZUKI extended warranty is equivalent to toilet tissue,they simply tell you its wear and tear. Now, i am short and the bike fits me well.It has good power,shifts good,on deep corners the floor boards scrape(oh well),handles good,and is pretty good on mileage(about 280 km a tank).Had my 1st issue in ecuador,gauges kept dying, and it took the dealer 2 days to find a bare wire in the steering column(50$),i used a k and n filter-sewrviced as required,AND BASICALLY I LOVE THE BIKE...but at 80,000 km it all falls apart-started using engine oil 1 liter for 800 km, front forks leaking,transmission clutch had to be replaced,and worst of all the fuel injection module packed it in(1500$)-ALL NOT COVERTED BY BUMPER TO BUMPER 3 YEAR SUZUKI EXTENDED WARRANTY-AND THE SELLING DEALER WAS NOOOOO HELP. So, fixed everything for 5000$ and ending up riding it till 140,000 km and sold it for 1700$. That was 2 years ago. 2 months ago I HAD TO RIDE SO I BOUGHT A 750 SHADOW AERO used a 2008, 40k,for 3800$ fully loaded with everything-it was SLOW,HANDLES LIKE A PIG, NO POWER,AND FORGET THE CHOKE ADJUSTMENT EVERY MORNING...SOLD IT IN 1 MONTH AND BOUGHT A USED 2012 SUZUKI C50T with 3800km a plane ride away(divorce sale) for 5250$-a good deal, triton blue and white,HANDLES GREAT,RIDS GREAT,IT SERIOUSLY NEEDS A DOUBLE FRONT DISK BRAKE AND REAR DISK(you just cant stop in an emergency)but,it looks wonderful rides beautifully and im extremely happy with it-the 2016 no longer had the BOULEVARD CHROME EMBLEM-SUZUKI EXECS YOUR NUTS...it now has a painted letterhead exactly like the shadow honda-suzuki-STOP FOLLOWING HONDA YOU HAVE A BETTER BIKE...CAPICHE?anyway..i plan to keep it and am now saving up for repairs at 80,000 km...but the bike is worth it, the 2006 i paid 11,000$ for...this one was like new less than half...write me if you need to talk...I HIGHLY RECOMMEND THE BIKE...BUT IT AINT NO HARLEY-EVEN THO IT LOOKS LIKE ONE.JOHN WAYNE

author: mike
text: Hi, I´m thinking about buying a new 2008 DR-Z400SM, but the local dealer doesn´t have one in stock. So, I have few questions. Are the spark plugs readily accessible or do you have to remove the gas tank just to change a plug? Also, is there a dipstick to check engine oil? Last, is there any reason why I should buy one?

author: Mike P.
text: Yes Mike you must pull the gas tank to change spark plugs. The oil dip stick is up on the right hand side of the frame up by the front forks as the dip stick comes off the oil cooler, not down on the motor.  In my opinion the best reason to buy a DRZ400SM is the 310 lbs weight of the bike lets you get through traffic like nothing. The bad side of the bike is (1) it´s not as easy to get all parts for as everybody tells you, (2) Not all Suzuki dealers are created equal, some are good, others are total zeros.  My local Suzuki dealer only cares about big $$$ Cruisers, not my little DRZ.  I found a Suzuki moto cross racer, this guy knows his stuff on my bike, him I can deal with.

author: mike
text: Hi, I´m thinking about buying a new 2008 DR-Z400SM, but the local dealer doesn´t have one in stock. So, I have few questions. Are the spark plugs readily accessible or do you have to remove the gas tank just to change a plug? Also, is there a dipstick to check engine oil? Last, is there any reason why I should buy one?

author: Mike P.
text: Yes Mike you must pull the gas tank to change spark plugs. The oil dip stick is up on the right hand side of the frame up by the front forks as the dip stick comes off the oil cooler, not down on the motor.  In my opinion the best reason to buy a DRZ400SM is the 310 lbs weight of the bike lets you get through traffic like nothing. The bad side of the bike is (1) it´s not as easy to get all parts for as everybody tells you, (2) Not all Suzuki dealers are created equal, some are good, others are total zeros.  My local Suzuki dealer only cares about big $$$ Cruisers, not my little DRZ.  I found a Suzuki moto cross racer, this guy knows his stuff on my bike, him I can deal with.

author: KMS
text: This winter I bought a 2006 Suzuki DR650 with only 500 miles that was running great a few weeks ago but now will not start. There is no owners manual so I do not know the proper starting proceedure and is frustrating the heck out of me. It has fuel (new) and the battery stays on a tender. All the lights and nuetral indicator works properly when the key is turned on. The starter sounds normal and battery strength seems normal but may need to be tested - engine turns and attempts to start but it will not turn over. I thought I was flooding it because the exhaust pipe and bike in general smells like gas so I think it is getting flooded or rich on gas. I removed the plugs and replaced with identical NKG plugs. Tomorrow I will double check the fuel line and start fiddling with the carb. There is not a big enough hill to get a rolling start or enough momentum to jump it off. Please reply if you have any ideas or if you can tell me proper starting procedure and how to use the choke, I would appreciate it. THank you for your consideration . . . Tony

author: Geordiem3
text: Hi My name is Garry and I just sold my zx9r b1 to buy my Gsxr1000k2 and Cannot get my heAd around what a huge leap bikes have taken within ten years !!! Although I have ridden a brand new one which is the same age difference between my zx9 and my Gsxr and I think they have slowed down !!! Doh ! I know my 02 model is a long stroke engine and the newer ones a shorter stroke! The reason I love the k2 is cause of the long stroke it has incredible power on tap from 2500 rpms where as with the new model it is quite tame till 8-9000rpm then it is like all hell is breaking loose with a huge surge of power much like a rz500 two stroke! My zx9r was the same too ! Tame to say the least ! The 02 Gsxr is a angry beast of a machine with power to burn like the zx9r after 8-9000 rpm but from 2000 rpm! I love and respect her !!! She is a beast not to be pushed hard by the inexperienced! They do bite lol! But what fun if you open her up ! Wow! I don´t believe after ridding a new 1000 and 1300 busa , it´s just not ten grands more bike is all ! My 02 has only 30k on odometer! I´ll be keeping my 02 model !

author: Geordiem3
text: As long as your revs aren´t too high it will be fine ! These bikes have a butterfly valve in the exhaust system kinda under your rh peg which helps burn unburnt gas and prevents backfires ! It also apparently prevents a torque dip between 4-4500 rpm! I labour mine down through the gears Constantly cause it sounds so tough and smooth with the scorpion muffler I have on mine ! Not to mention how good the  blue from the titanium looks on a blue bike !! I have been riding for over 25 yrs now and using my gears to brake has never harmed any of my over 40 bikes!!! Go for it! Just make sure your oil and coolant is good and don´t rev above the suggested redline when decelerating !!! Hope it puts your mind at ease ! It´s called engine braking !!!! Cheers Garry !!

author: Afzal Mahammad
text: Phenomenal power with perfect stability and this bike is remarkably agile and nimble. By far the most exciting and practical road-bike available.  Power: 5/5 (staggering) Performance: 5/5 (sensational) Stability: 5/5 (perfectly balanced) Comfort: 4/5 Instrumentation: 4/5 Usability: 5/5 Economy: 4/5 (high revving does kill-off the fuel!) Ergonomics: 4/5 (seat forces you to lean slightly forwards against tank) Rideability: 5/5 (so easy to ride, jump on and go!)

author: A Zater
text: Picked up a new Ecstar Double R last week. In this one week of ownership, the bike has thoroughly impressed every sense in my being. This is my second GSXR-1000. My first GSXR was a Mladin edition K4. The handling and power delivery is out of this world. I have never experienced such a finely tuned factory/stock bike. The up/down clutchless shifter works flawlessly. I have huge plans with many years of performance planned for this bike. This is a GSXR that has stepped up to regain its title as king of superbikes.

Thank you for any information you can share. Chris Model: 2002 Suzuki GSXR 750 Miles 11,000 Upgrades include: power commander, velocity stacks, a CBM racing air filter, yoshimura carbon fiber exhaust, new brakes and rotars, new throttle body, upgraded steering damper, LED light kit, fender eliminator, feame sliders, led turn signals and brake lights, tinted double bubble windscreen, new master fuse, gel seats, new battery, extra battery with tender. I also have an additional tinted windscreen, new fairings and tailpiece that can be custom painted any way you like so you can make the bike your own. Cons: only issues are cosmetic, some chips and scratches etc, but it comes with a whole additional body kit so it will make up for the blemishes and you get to customize it the way you want.

author: Colin Medler.
text: Do yourself a favour buy a standard busa not a limited edition, Heres why.. the finish is poor on all the bikes. stay away from the black ones they look terrible if you look closely. go to the show room and look closely at even a brand new one, the marks in the paint are unreal. I´ve never seen a tidy limited edition. depends on how picky you are. I´ve got blue and silver on a 54 plate from new and you really have to work hard to keep them looking good. the finish on the tank is shocking. buy a baglux tank case.. about £60. handles very well on the road, its extra weight on the road and slightly softer suspension makes other sports riders wonder how you do it... their light and hard on suspension making them twitchy...  on the track they´ll kill you!!! Dont believe otherwise. the busa is an awesome road bike. not a track tool.. I´ve had gsxr 6& 750. Brakes. get them sorted. braided hoses and uprated pads. service cost are very reasonable. dont believe the articles about tyre wear... its ten times worse!!! Chain snapping rumours?? never met a busa owner who´s had that problem. The vibration at 4 to 5000 rpm is bloody anoying on the motorway. it vibrates at the perfrect cruising speed... sometimes they struggle to start when they are hot. they all do it and theres nothing you can do about it. right. thats the negatives out the way. I´ve owned many and ridden hundreds more. Dont be a %&#!¤. buy one. they are the bollocks!!

That bike is heavy for sure but I like it than nothing else because it can go fast and reliable.But if you had no bike before buy somthing like suzuki gsxr 600 of 750.They are perfect to get ready for a monster like Hayabusa

author: Steve
text: I would say: Bad idea. Buy something in the 600cc range and learn it well. Then...when you’ve mastered low speed skills, cornering skills and rider knowledge...then buy a Hayabusa. I think that this is just too big, cumbersome at low speeds and powerful to be a good bike to learn on. Bad idea. I’ve been riding for over 50 years and love my Busa. But I would not recommend trying to learn all there is to know on a bike like this. Get something easier (and more forgiving) to handle. Steve

author: shawn f
text: Riding on the hayabusa was a different and thrilling experience, as compared to my former 2002 R1.  like a double-edged sword, it offers the stability at high speed, yet it also proves too bulky at low speed.  Just a matter of time to getting used to it, and once u´ve passed that adaptation period, the road is for you to roam. however there is one problem which i encountered.. the idling rev of the machine drops every few weeks, despite constant calibration to maintain idling rev at 1 to 1.1.  It has puzzled me, and there are people suggesting installing PC3 or voltage to overcome this problem. till now, i am still sticking to the conventional way of calibrating it every few weeks.  thats all from me.

author: Colin Medler.
text: Do yourself a favour buy a standard busa not a limited edition, Heres why.. the finish is poor on all the bikes. stay away from the black ones they look terrible if you look closely. go to the show room and look closely at even a brand new one, the marks in the paint are unreal. I´ve never seen a tidy limited edition. depends on how picky you are. I´ve got blue and silver on a 54 plate from new and you really have to work hard to keep them looking good. the finish on the tank is shocking. buy a baglux tank case.. about £60. handles very well on the road, its extra weight on the road and slightly softer suspension makes other sports riders wonder how you do it... their light and hard on suspension making them twitchy...  on the track they´ll kill you!!! Dont believe otherwise. the busa is an awesome road bike. not a track tool.. I´ve had gsxr 6& 750. Brakes. get them sorted. braided hoses and uprated pads. service cost are very reasonable. dont believe the articles about tyre wear... its ten times worse!!! Chain snapping rumours?? never met a busa owner who´s had that problem. The vibration at 4 to 5000 rpm is bloody anoying on the motorway. it vibrates at the perfrect cruising speed... sometimes they struggle to start when they are hot. they all do it and theres nothing you can do about it. right. thats the negatives out the way. I´ve owned many and ridden hundreds more. Dont be a %&#!¤. buy one. they are the bollocks!!

author: Steve
text: I would say: Bad idea. Buy something in the 600cc range and learn it well. Then...when you’ve mastered low speed skills, cornering skills and rider knowledge...then buy a Hayabusa. I think that this is just too big, cumbersome at low speeds and powerful to be a good bike to learn on. Bad idea. I’ve been riding for over 50 years and love my Busa. But I would not recommend trying to learn all there is to know on a bike like this. Get something easier (and more forgiving) to handle. Steve

author: shawn f
text: Riding on the hayabusa was a different and thrilling experience, as compared to my former 2002 R1.  like a double-edged sword, it offers the stability at high speed, yet it also proves too bulky at low speed.  Just a matter of time to getting used to it, and once u´ve passed that adaptation period, the road is for you to roam. however there is one problem which i encountered.. the idling rev of the machine drops every few weeks, despite constant calibration to maintain idling rev at 1 to 1.1.  It has puzzled me, and there are people suggesting installing PC3 or voltage to overcome this problem. till now, i am still sticking to the conventional way of calibrating it every few weeks.  thats all from me.

author: Chief Big Smoke
text: This bike has SHOWA suspension right out of the box. Also, a well spread out, 4 speed gear box alows you to really use the increadible powerband to its max potential. Trust me, you will most likely never touch 4th gear on any race track, and rarely need 1st. So think of this bike as a Two Speed. Fast, and Very Fast. My last bike, a 98´ Yamaha YZ 125, had 6 speeds. But it only had about 28 HP. My (stock) RMZ has about 52 HP. And with an alluminum frame, this bike is almost as light as a 250 2 stroke. I HIGHLY Recommend the 05´ Suzuki RMZ 450 to any experienced riders. New riders... I hope you have Medical insurrence.

author: moto mom
text: Just purchased a used dirt bike for my son and he rode it last weekend and all was good.  Then he noticed a vibration in engine (you can feel it when your gripping the throttle) but doesn´t do it when it is in gear...took it to extreme powersports to have them look at it.  Said it could be the crank bearing and would cost me over 350 to have it fixed...I have been doing a lot of research and there are so many things it could be.  Any help or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.  I think they are just wanting the work and im going to get screwed...I´m going to pick it up tomorrow and bring it home without letting them change the bearing in it...How do i go about trouble shooting a problem like this.  PS...i am going to school to become a motorcycle mechanic but just started so my knowledge is limited. author: badtoad100
text: Wanted to get back into Shape and picked up a 2000 XR200 for me and the2006 RM 85 for my 15yr old. She Was Not able to ride the RM it was Way TO fast for her. I was not happy with the XR but Found I loved the way the RM felt. I am short Fat and out of shape 5ft 6in and 220lb.The XR did not handle that good on the big hits but this little bike was outstanding with me on it. I picked it up from a racer I don’t know if the Suspension had mods but Works well for me in the woods. Now I have made some Mods to make it work for me. From Ebay used PR2 porting and Head mod making it a 105cc (did Case Mod Myself) FMF Fatty Pipe silencer combo Flat slide Carb 28 mm (2hrs Fine tune Jetting) K&N Filter V Force 3 reed Cage and intake Boot Mod And since I run it in tight Trails where you have to always be turning Jumping or something You have to climb over is in your way I do not need Speed I Geared it Down to 11 52 gearing with a O-ring chain. Now I don’t know Anny other mods I can do but it works well for me I can jump a 35ft double in 4 gear I can climb rocks in first trail riding I am in 2nd thru 5thmost of the time almost never get it out in the open to hit 6th but pulls well when I do Motocross track I am in 2nd 3rd and 4th on the small Track I have been on. I know I should be on a bigger Bike But I really like this One and it fits my size and I Feel Better with it and Go faster than on a bigger bike Like my Brothers YZ 400

author: Fellow Traveller
text: OK, I´ll start this off. Just bought an ´09 DL1000 after researching the heck out of it. Get these add-ons as soon as possible, in this order of importance: (1) Engine guards (crash bars)-- Three or four makes out there, and each has good and less good points. I went with the Pat Walsh Designs bars + bash plate + oil cooler screen combo. Others look quite decent, too, however. The big point here is that this bike is tall. At some point it´s going to go over; I don´t mean in a road crash, but in your driveway at one or two mph when you´re not paying quite enough attention backing it up (that would be me), or when the kickstand punches through some soft asphalt (like the Harley guy I saw in a cafe parking lot one hot summer day; he was not happy to hear his heap o´ chrome apparently pass out and tip over as he was walking away, trust me). In any case, the engine guard will save you big bux if your Vee heels over. (2) Center stand (Suzuki´s is a wallet-numbing $239; try SW Motech -- brutally strong, works fine, costs significantly less. Some say the Motech has less ground clearance than the Suzuki stand; others say the Suzuki limits left turn lean. If I had to choose between those faults, I´ll take the vertical shortage any day vs. lean shortage. I can stand the rare rock bash, but having the stand jack your rear tire off the ground in a severe lean...not good. But this could be BS -- others say the Suzuki is fine in severe turns. Who knows. It was a money call for me, and I´m happy. (3) MadStad adjustable windshield mount -- The fact is, the windshield on this bike is very far forward of the rider and is a tiny-ass thing to boot, sort of a vestigial windscreen, so it just ends up producing nasty turbulence right where your head and upper body are. You can get an acceptable set-up by installing the MadStad and adjusting the stock screen so that it´s higher and lets air in underneath to kill the turbulence. Again, this is quite acceptable. But if you´re going to ride long distances, you´ll probably want to go with an aftermarket taller wider screen on that MadStad mount. Just saying. (4) Mirror extenders -- The fact is, unless your shoulders are about 12\

author: Steve
text: I´m a long term biker, now 44 and started on a Yamaha FS1E in 1978. I´ve had a lot of bikes from 50cc to 850cc and I´m an advanced rider. I´ve had 500cc mopeds, road bikes, dirt bikes, trials bikes, tourers and superbikes (bikes over 750cc that were fast in their day!). I had 4 at once, once - all different and I currently have a Chinese 200cc Honda 125 copy trials bike because it only cost me £800 new! My question is, having not really ridden modern era bikes from about 1985 onwards, I´m looking for a single bike that gives me a bit of everything. I like the fun of a trials bike (don´t need too much off road), the pleasure of touring and cruising at speed 2 up with luggage, the enjoyment of commuting past cars to work and the thrill of a bit of speed occasionally. I have a car too which I need for a baby on the way and I´m not a die hard biker. Wanting to spend between £2000 and £3500. I think what I need has to be the adventure type bikes (like the Transalp/Paris Dakar style) rather than a sports tourer (like the old CBR600/1000). The bigger the thirstier I guess, but I´ll brunt it as it needs to be/do everything. I realize I won´t squeeze it through any pinholes. So far it´s the Suzuki V Strom 1000 Grand Tourer - looks great!) and the uglier Honda XL1000, with a Yamaha TDM and the BMW (and the KLR?) as competitors. Any advice/experience to pass on? Thanks, Steve

text: To be curious about 2003 Suzuki V-strom 1000DL 0-100 km accerelation and top speed (KM) Plese help me... yours respectfully

author: Steve T.
text: I have not timed my V-Strom on a quarter mile track or even come close to exploring its top end, but I can give you my seat-of-the-pants experience.  At speeds exceeding 110 mph wind buffeting becomes extreme with the stock wind shield.  The bike was willing to keep accelerating, I wasn´t.  I suspect a taller, wider windshield would reduce this.  Acceleration is scary fast.  With the throttle wide open I can feel my butt start to slip over the seat step in any gear.  Even at partial throttle, passing other vehicles makes them look like they are braking, although I doubt they even know I am there until I pass.  The V-Strom is a high performance road bike, but be aware 2/3 of the weight is on the rear wheel.  This has made me afraid of wind.  Cross winds feel like they can blow the front wheel out from under me.  Head winds at high speed have caused the front wheel to flutter.  There is more to going fast than power.  V-Strom has the power, but not the balance.  I recommend you keep shopping.

author: Rich g
text: You´re absolutely right about the V-Strom.  But, its intentions are NOT for doing maximum speed.  It´s a BIG, comfortable sport-touring bike with some light duty trailing capability.  With its usable low/mid range torque, I can usually keep up with riders on sportbikes until I loose my ground clearence, while I´m sitting practically straight up! Strom is NOT a sportbike, it´s NOT setup to be a sportbike...

author: Steve T.
text: I have never rode a bike as wind sensitive as my V-Strom 1000.  A cross wind tries to blow the wheels out from under me.  Has anybody else experienced this?

text: You should change windblogger to something different.it will help you a little.

author: tom d
text: 2006 v strom 1000 25.000kl  in Nova Scotia C A N its allwas windy trust you ride and i found matzler tires help  p s two up with full paners and top case in the wind is fun . ride free!

author: IB1Kiwi
text: Have had a Strom for a bout 3 months now and clocked up a little over 8000km. Sooo easy to just keep rolling along (with the odd bit of twist and go to keep it interesting). Love how firm the bike feels up to it´s limits ans yes I´ve got the standard suspension just dialled up reasonably tight. One niggle with my bike is at low revs (circa 2800) and light throttle (town riding) I get surging, to the point of the occasional miss and even the odd backfire gets thrown in once in a while. Has anyone else experienced this and more importantly, is there a fix? Other than this I´ve got to say this bike has a grin factor like I´ve never had before, great job Suzuki!

author: Jay
text: Hi Jaca.  I´m new to this forum so my response is kind of old now.  Yes, the 2002 and 2006 mufflers / exhaust pipes are the same Suzuki part numbers.  Will interchange perfectly. Hope this helps 1 1/2 years later.

author: Fellow Traveller
text: OK, I´ll start this off. Just bought an ´09 DL1000 after researching the heck out of it. Get these add-ons as soon as possible, in this order of importance: (1) Engine guards (crash bars)-- Three or four makes out there, and each has good and less good points. I went with the Pat Walsh Designs bars + bash plate + oil cooler screen combo. Others look quite decent, too, however. The big point here is that this bike is tall. At some point it´s going to go over; I don´t mean in a road crash, but in your driveway at one or two mph when you´re not paying quite enough attention backing it up (that would be me), or when the kickstand punches through some soft asphalt (like the Harley guy I saw in a cafe parking lot one hot summer day; he was not happy to hear his heap o´ chrome apparently pass out and tip over as he was walking away, trust me). In any case, the engine guard will save you big bux if your Vee heels over. (2) Center stand (Suzuki´s is a wallet-numbing $239; try SW Motech -- brutally strong, works fine, costs significantly less. Some say the Motech has less ground clearance than the Suzuki stand; others say the Suzuki limits left turn lean. If I had to choose between those faults, I´ll take the vertical shortage any day vs. lean shortage. I can stand the rare rock bash, but having the stand jack your rear tire off the ground in a severe lean...not good. But this could be BS -- others say the Suzuki is fine in severe turns. Who knows. It was a money call for me, and I´m happy. (3) MadStad adjustable windshield mount -- The fact is, the windshield on this bike is very far forward of the rider and is a tiny-ass thing to boot, sort of a vestigial windscreen, so it just ends up producing nasty turbulence right where your head and upper body are. You can get an acceptable set-up by installing the MadStad and adjusting the stock screen so that it´s higher and lets air in underneath to kill the turbulence. Again, this is quite acceptable. But if you´re going to ride long distances, you´ll probably want to go with an aftermarket taller wider screen on that MadStad mount. Just saying. (4) Mirror extenders -- The fact is, unless your shoulders are about 12\

author: Bub
text: I own a 2006 VStrom, have very few problems.  Mainly tire wear, I run street tires and only 3 to 4 thousand miles on them. Used Dunlop, Avon, Conti and am now on Bridgestone.  Only changes are a 45 tooth rear sproket and custom exhaust and Power Commander. I live in S.C. and ride the mountain roads.

author: bobbyvstrom
text: Bub is right that it´s a great bike, though I got 12,000 miles on my rear and have over 19,000 on the front tire.  This bike should not be rated with sport bikes or naked bikes.  It´s a dual-sport or Adventure bike.  At the rallys, lots of riders have knobbies on them with bash plates and such for rough off-roading.  I´ve ridden it on two, 4,000 mile plus trips, two-up, with luggage and camping gear. It´s a great all-around bike. I love it!

author: Phil Lynch
text: I was a previous owner of a 2004 DL1000 Vstrom. Rode it from Tucson,AZ to Cabo San Lucas in Baja, Mexico. Most of the roads were well paved, but one road turned out to be a major headache. It was rough all the way. Took over 1 day to go one hundred miles. The bike handled okay, but the supsension took a beating. This bike is for fire roads only. Recently purchased a 2007 DL1000 Vstrom. Minor improvements have been noted to the fuel injection system, overdrive gearing and transmission shifting. Extremely happy with this bike. I get around 45-50 mpg riding outside of Tucson. Would probably get 40-45 mpg if ridden hard. Yes, this bike should come with a better windshield, centerstand, heated grips, etc. But then again, these item are available on the aftermarket. Lost of good stuff to choose from. Any questions, just ask.

text: I did a very silly thing -had the cover off inspecting the condition of the front sprocket. Having cleaned off the chain-flung gungeand detemined that thesprocket was serviceable,  I reassembled but put the spacer that should be for the front bolt of the hydraulic  unit  on to the push rod for the clutch release. When I tightened up the bolts, the spacer popped out the rubber seal for the push rod allowing the engine oil to escape -ugh, I was weary at the time. ? IS THIS A COMPLETE ENGINE SPLIT AND STRIP ? or can it be repaired more easily. My lovely ´03 V-Strom DL1000 is caput at the minute. Mike, Dublin ,Ireland

author: jase bond
text: hey man! glad to see your weighing out your options and trying to get informed the best u can.Personally i spent 3 months of hard looking at all sorts of motorcyclea.I knew that i wanted a dual purpose,or atleast capable of going of road (gravel-hard packed dirt etc. and would allow me to carry a large load.At first i looked at the gs 1200,loved the bike,well everything except the price tag.Really couldnt justify spending 24000 (tax,bags included) so i began looking at something with all the same characteristics and i came across the vstrom 1000.Did look at the tiger,cappnacord,and ktm,and non of those seemed to measure up to the strom in my own opinion.COst was a factor for me,but i would spend more if i was getting a far better machine,and i have to say the dl1000 strom hands down was the best choice.I now have a 2007 sitting in my garage (heated grips,givi crash bars,tank bra) extras and i love it.No regrets, but do your own investigations mate, go to the v strom forum online and there u can research anything u want on the bike and ask questions,but i suggest going through the forum as it is very informative to say the least.Happy hunting and good luck!!!

author: jac
text: Thank you very much Jase, I´m really appreciate your words. Regards.

author: Steve
text: I´m a long term biker, now 44 and started on a Yamaha FS1E in 1978. I´ve had a lot of bikes from 50cc to 850cc and I´m an advanced rider. I´ve had 500cc mopeds, road bikes, dirt bikes, trials bikes, tourers and superbikes (bikes over 750cc that were fast in their day!). I had 4 at once, once - all different and I currently have a Chinese 200cc Honda 125 copy trials bike because it only cost me £800 new! My question is, having not really ridden modern era bikes from about 1985 onwards, I´m looking for a single bike that gives me a bit of everything. I like the fun of a trials bike (don´t need too much off road), the pleasure of touring and cruising at speed 2 up with luggage, the enjoyment of commuting past cars to work and the thrill of a bit of speed occasionally. I have a car too which I need for a baby on the way and I´m not a die hard biker. Wanting to spend between £2000 and £3500. I think what I need has to be the adventure type bikes (like the Transalp/Paris Dakar style) rather than a sports tourer (like the old CBR600/1000). The bigger the thirstier I guess, but I´ll brunt it as it needs to be/do everything. I realize I won´t squeeze it through any pinholes. So far it´s the Suzuki V Strom 1000 Grand Tourer - looks great!) and the uglier Honda XL1000, with a Yamaha TDM and the BMW (and the KLR?) as competitors. Any advice/experience to pass on? Thanks, Steve

author: Bazza
text: I have a KLV 1000 which is baically the same bike with kawasaki badges I have the same fault it is a fueling problem just back from dealer´s and the have made it worse.I am phoning kawasaki uk tommorow.The mechanic apparently said all the bikes have the same fault I find this very hard to believe and if so you would think they would have a fix for it by now.I will post again once I get a responce

author: Bazza
text: My Dealer cant fix the problem as it is down to the fueling which has been set up to give the best emmisions,so it can pass the euro 3 laws.The head mechanic said the only way to fix it is to fit a power commander at my own expense which is £280 so have contacted dynojet uk.It is either that or I sell the bike and loose a fortune

author: James Dizes
text: I bought a brand new 2005 v strom 1000 last year and I had the same problem at the same rpm´s, I had my Suzuki dealer look at it and they saw that the fuel mixture was too lean to comply with smog controls, the dealer installed a tecklusion fuel injection device and it fixed the problem. If you need more information you can e mail me James

author: Jim
text: I have owned a number of bikes in the past including a BMW1100GS, in my opionion the V-strom out performs all, hands down. It is a tall bike, and I can not comment on how handling may change if lowered. My wife and I have traveled on every possible road condition, including gravel and sand, I can honestly say that the bike handles as well two up as it does when solo. Even though it is classified as a dual sport, its 80% road 20% dirt, it handles wonderfully on twisty roads. I do have side as well as top case, all Givi. Jim

author: Steve T.
text: I have not timed my V-Strom on a quarter mile track or even come close to exploring its top end, but I can give you my seat-of-the-pants experience.  At speeds exceeding 110 mph wind buffeting becomes extreme with the stock wind shield.  The bike was willing to keep accelerating, I wasn´t.  I suspect a taller, wider windshield would reduce this.  Acceleration is scary fast.  With the throttle wide open I can feel my butt start to slip over the seat step in any gear.  Even at partial throttle, passing other vehicles makes them look like they are braking, although I doubt they even know I am there until I pass.  The V-Strom is a high performance road bike, but be aware 2/3 of the weight is on the rear wheel.  This has made me afraid of wind.  Cross winds feel like they can blow the front wheel out from under me.  Head winds at high speed have caused the front wheel to flutter.  There is more to going fast than power.  V-Strom has the power, but not the balance.  I recommend you keep shopping.

It was rough all the way. Took over 1 day to go one hundred miles. The bike handled okay, but the supsension took a beating. This bike is for fire roads only. Recently purchased a 2007 DL1000 Vstrom. Minor improvements have been noted to the fuel injection system, overdrive gearing and transmission shifting. Extremely happy with this bike. I get around 45-50 mpg riding outside of Tucson. Would probably get 40-45 mpg if ridden hard. Yes, this bike should come with a better windshield, centerstand, heated grips, etc. But then again, these item are available on the aftermarket. Lost of good stuff to choose from. Any questions, just ask.

author: jase bond
text: hey man! glad to see your weighing out your options and trying to get informed the best u can.Personally i spent 3 months of hard looking at all sorts of motorcyclea.I knew that i wanted a dual purpose,or atleast capable of going of road (gravel-hard packed dirt etc. and would allow me to carry a large load.At first i looked at the gs 1200,loved the bike,well everything except the price tag.Really couldnt justify spending 24000 (tax,bags included) so i began looking at something with all the same characteristics and i came across the vstrom 1000.Did look at the tiger,cappnacord,and ktm,and non of those seemed to measure up to the strom in my own opinion.COst was a factor for me,but i would spend more if i was getting a far better machine,and i have to say the dl1000 strom hands down was the best choice.I now have a 2007 sitting in my garage (heated grips,givi crash bars,tank bra) extras and i love it.No regrets, but do your own investigations mate, go to the v strom forum online and there u can research anything u want on the bike and ask questions,but i suggest going through the forum as it is very informative to say the least.Happy hunting and good luck!!!

author: Steve
text: I´m a long term biker, now 44 and started on a Yamaha FS1E in 1978. I´ve had a lot of bikes from 50cc to 850cc and I´m an advanced rider. I´ve had 500cc mopeds, road bikes, dirt bikes, trials bikes, tourers and superbikes (bikes over 750cc that were fast in their day!). I had 4 at once, once - all different and I currently have a Chinese 200cc Honda 125 copy trials bike because it only cost me £800 new! My question is, having not really ridden modern era bikes from about 1985 onwards, I´m looking for a single bike that gives me a bit of everything. I like the fun of a trials bike (don´t need too much off road), the pleasure of touring and cruising at speed 2 up with luggage, the enjoyment of commuting past cars to work and the thrill of a bit of speed occasionally. I have a car too which I need for a baby on the way and I´m not a die hard biker. Wanting to spend between £2000 and £3500. I think what I need has to be the adventure type bikes (like the Transalp/Paris Dakar style) rather than a sports tourer (like the old CBR600/1000). The bigger the thirstier I guess, but I´ll brunt it as it needs to be/do everything. I realize I won´t squeeze it through any pinholes. So far it´s the Suzuki V Strom 1000 Grand Tourer - looks great!) and the uglier Honda XL1000, with a Yamaha TDM and the BMW (and the KLR?) as competitors. Any advice/experience to pass on? Thanks, Steve

author: Steve T.
text: I have not timed my V-Strom on a quarter mile track or even come close to exploring its top end, but I can give you my seat-of-the-pants experience.  At speeds exceeding 110 mph wind buffeting becomes extreme with the stock wind shield.  The bike was willing to keep accelerating, I wasn´t.  I suspect a taller, wider windshield would reduce this.  Acceleration is scary fast.  With the throttle wide open I can feel my butt start to slip over the seat step in any gear.  Even at partial throttle, passing other vehicles makes them look like they are braking, although I doubt they even know I am there until I pass.  The V-Strom is a high performance road bike, but be aware 2/3 of the weight is on the rear wheel.  This has made me afraid of wind.  Cross winds feel like they can blow the front wheel out from under me.  Head winds at high speed have caused the front wheel to flutter.  There is more to going fast than power.  V-Strom has the power, but not the balance.  I recommend you keep shopping.

author: Rich g
text: You´re absolutely right about the V-Strom.  But, its intentions are NOT for doing maximum speed.  It´s a BIG, comfortable sport-touring bike with some light duty trailing capability.  With its usable low/mid range torque, I can usually keep up with riders on sportbikes until I loose my ground clearence, while I´m sitting practically straight up! Strom is NOT a sportbike, it´s NOT setup to be a sportbike...

author: Ian Scott
text: WEE STROM  .. USER TEST Rider :-  46 years , 5´ 9´´   ,    85 kg ....      17 years riding on different bikes. Wee strom mileage = 1500  miles Ok  so  its my turn to repay the debt .  Before buying my wee strom I read just about every review or test on the web ..  so here are my payback thoughts ---- I am not a Suzuki dealer, rep or bike journalist, just a plain old user living in Chile.….. First impressions are often correct  (said Talking Heads many moons ago ) and mine were and are still very very positive ..  this bike is fast, comfy (although the seat is only good for about an hour), and handles superbly. I love the light weight, good brakes, RESPONSIVE ENGINE and upright ergonomics.. This is a modern bike….  What I mean to say is that it makes my sporster appear like from the 15 century… .  everything is totally modern and up to date. This is to say everything works efficiently .. The 650 is an engine that likes to rev ….  If  your in love with the thump thump sound of a low reving bike then this is perhaps not the machine for you … personally  I  love the thump thump of british twins …  but for me the wee stroms  other gleaming characteristics make me forgive its racing bike sound which can be best described as muted in standard form .. so much so that the slight whine of the fuel pump can be heard whilst on the move ….. one of the notable aspects of the sound is that closing the throttle it sounds like a jet engine !   ….  You know when your coming into land and just before touchdown the pilot shuts back ….. its that whistly whine sound …   I am actually learning to like it !  being so quiet has its advantages of course,  I don’t annoy the neighbours late at night, and I can pull up in front of everyone sitting outside the pub and not create a scandal .. Its  a revy engine ….  Cruiser lovers  might begrudge the relatively high revs at cruising speed 5,000 = 70mph    6,000 = 80mph  indicated …. I often  feel for another top gear at these speeds on the motorway to bring the revs down !   and then I think “well it doesn’t matter because gas milage is good anyway ”  so a 6th gear indicator would be very useful...  that’s not to say I am a real plodder ….  your talking to a guy that took his sportster back into the shop because the engine was cutting out  ….   Only to be told by a startled dealer that the bike was fine ,,, it was just the rev limiter cutting  in all the time ! A downside to these quite high cruising revs is a buzzing in the handlebar …  after a 2 hr trip on a motorway last week, my right hand had “pins and needles” upon dismounting. This was partly cured by wearing a more padded glove in the palm area … I am thinking about putting tape or something around the handlebar mount to insulate this buzz … Ok now the screen ….  I actually like the screen !   after loads of naked bikes its my first time with a piece of plastic out front ….  And it helps …  its not perfect ….  But then again if you want to ride in a pocket of still air …  then buy an outright tourer …. Or maybe buy a larger aftermarket screen …  I have tried the 3 positions and the highest position I feel offers the least buffeting (and most protection). At this height I can just see above it to give a clear view of the road  although in urban surroundings the screen at this height would have to be clean because objects at less than 8 meters distance will be seen throught  it and not over it. I am contemplating taking it off altogether for city riding for this reason.  Interestingly if at speed I lean forwards to the point that my chest would be resting on a tank bag , then the buffeting ceases, but of course neck angle increases a little …. Perhaps Suzuki could perhaps move the screen back say six inches --- it would make all the difference, the screen feels just a little far forwards … Ergonomics and seat height I love the big chasis. On Chilean bad potholed roads a large bike offers more stability. It is a tall bike,  I can get on and off without problems whilist there is no bag strapped onto the rear seat … if there is then I need to find a kerb! Or develop a leg 90 degree cocked dismount (requires a little practice!) At a standstill both feet make ball contact. Handling Superb: I can point the bike at a bend and it holds the line no problem and is confidence inspiring … its easy to flick around with the wide bars .. the tyres are fine too … I have a couple of times locked the rear wheel on a panic stop situation in urban traffic .. nothing scary and was easily corrected. After the harley I am not used to having a responsive back brake ! In respect to the width of the bike,  … I have to think twice before paddling my way between two lines of cars at a traffic jam.  Will I get through ? on my old SRX600 the widest thing was my elbows, the wee strom has bars, and mirrors to calculate and the later tends to be at the exact same height as many 4X4 wing mirrors.. …  not a huge problem … I just have to think twice that’s all .. .. the suspension is light years better than my harley --- although experts have sounded its shortfalls …  I find it adequate … maybe a little rough but fine. Ground clearance is superb ..  no grounding of the pegs yet ….. the lights are great ,  the 190kg is fantastic, easy to roll in and out of the garage, off road ??? I agree with most testers,  its fine on most gravel and dry dirt roads ,,,  not so hot on deep mug or sand … Would I buy this bike again = yes definitely !! self canceling indicators would also be nice I got back on my sportster the other day and it was like getting into a 1920s ford T …  very nice to look at but …..

author: Ian Scott
text: WEE STROM .. USER TEST Rider :- 46 years , 5´ 9´´ , 85 kg .... 17 years riding on different bikes. Wee strom mileage = 1500 miles Ok so its my turn to repay the debt . Before buying my wee strom I read just about every review or test on the web .. so here are my payback thoughts ---- I am not a Suzuki dealer, rep or bike journalist, just a plain old user living in Chile.….. First impressions are often correct (said Talking Heads many moons ago ) and mine were and are still very very positive .. this bike is fast, comfy (although the seat is only good for about an hour), and handles superbly. I love the light weight, good brakes, RESPONSIVE ENGINE and upright ergonomics.. This is a modern bike…. What I mean to say is that it makes my sporster appear like from the 15 century… . everything is totally modern and up to date. This is to say everything works efficiently .. The 650 is an engine that likes to rev …. If your in love with the thump thump sound of a low reving bike then this is perhaps not the machine for you … personally I love the thump thump of british twins … but for me the wee stroms other gleaming characteristics make me forgive its racing bike sound which can be best described as muted in standard form .. so much so that the slight whine of the fuel pump can be heard whilst on the move ….. one of the notable aspects of the sound is that closing the throttle it sounds like a jet engine ! …. You know when your coming into land and just before touchdown the pilot shuts back ….. its that whistly whine sound … I am actually learning to like it ! being so quiet has its advantages of course, I don’t annoy the neighbours late at night, and I can pull up in front of everyone sitting outside the pub and not create a scandal .. Its a revy engine …. Cruiser lovers might begrudge the relatively high revs at cruising speed 5,000 = 70mph 6,000 = 80mph indicated …. I often feel for another top gear at these speeds on the motorway to bring the revs down ! and then I think “well it doesn’t matter because gas milage is good anyway ” so a 6th gear indicator would be very useful... that’s not to say I am a real plodder …. your talking to a guy that took his sportster back into the shop because the engine was cutting out …. Only to be told by a startled dealer that the bike was fine ,,, it was just the rev limiter cutting in all the time ! author: Mark Cook
text: If you want less torque and better gas mileage, a 16 or 17 tooth counter sprocket is best.  If you want quicker acceleration and more torque, a 14 tooth counter sprocket would be best.

author: Vibes
text: I have owned many Bikes over the years the 650 v-strom is the best to date as an all rounder´ I have one big problem with it as Iam cruising along at around 100 KLM their is an enoying vibration in the handelbars (throttle)The Bike has done over 10,000 KLM, the service is due at 12,000 I have asked a few people about my problem & they say it could be that the throttle bodies are out of sinc & thats causing the vibes , I said that to the dealer & he swept it under the carpet but did say on the 12,000 KLM service the do check the throttle bodies then. The Bike shop Iam enoyed at because they did not check the throttle bodies at the 6,000KLM service when I mentioned the vibes I was having maybe its a big job ha, the bike is still under warranty, is their any one out their with the same problem??? Cheers Julian.

Only to be told by a startled dealer that the bike was fine ,,, it was just the rev limiter cutting in all the time ! A downside to these quite high cruising revs is a buzzing in the handlebar … after a 2 hr trip on a motorway last week, my right hand had “pins and needles” upon dismounting. This was partly cured by wearing a more padded glove in the palm area … I am thinking about putting tape or something around the handlebar mount to insulate this buzz … Ok now the screen …. I actually like the screen ! after loads of naked bikes its my first time with a piece of plastic out front …. And it helps … its not perfect …. But then again if you want to ride in a pocket of still air … then buy an outright tourer …. Or maybe buy a larger aftermarket screen … I have tried the 3 positions and the highest position I feel offers the least buffeting (and most protection). At this height I can just see above it to give a clear view of the road although in urban surroundings the screen at this height would have to be clean because objects at less than 8 meters distance will be seen throught it and not over it. I am contemplating taking it off altogether for city riding for this reason. Interestingly if at speed I lean forwards to the point that my chest would be resting on a tank bag , then the buffeting ceases, but of course neck angle increases a little …. Perhaps Suzuki could perhaps move the screen back say six inches --- it would make all the difference, the screen feels just a little far forwards … Ergonomics and seat height I love the big chasis. On Chilean bad potholed roads a large bike offers more stability. It is a tall bike, I can get on and off without problems whilist there is no bag strapped onto the rear seat … if there is then I need to find a kerb! Or develop a leg 90 degree cocked dismount (requires a little practice!) At a standstill both feet make ball contact. Handling Superb: I can point the bike at a bend and it holds the line no problem and is confidence inspiring … its easy to flick around with the wide bars .. the tyres are fine too … I have a couple of times locked the rear wheel on a panic stop situation in urban traffic .. nothing scary and was easily corrected. After the harley I am not used to having a responsive back brake ! In respect to the width of the bike, … I have to think twice before paddling my way between two lines of cars at a traffic jam. Will I get through ? on my old SRX600 the widest thing was my elbows, the wee strom has bars, and mirrors to calculate and the later tends to be at the exact same height as many 4X4 wing mirrors.. … not a huge problem … I just have to think twice that’s all .. .. the suspension is light years better than my harley --- although experts have sounded its shortfalls … I find it adequate … maybe a little rough but fine. Ground clearance is superb .. no grounding of the pegs yet ….. the lights are great , the 190kg is fantastic, easy to roll in and out of the garage, off road ??? I agree with most testers, its fine on most gravel and dry dirt roads ,,, not so hot on deep mug or sand … Would I buy this bike again = yes definitely !! self canceling indicators would also be nice I got back on my sportster the other day and it was like getting into a 1920s ford T … very nice to look at but …..

text: If you want less torque and better gas mileage, a 16 or 17 tooth counter sprocket is best.  If you want quicker acceleration and more torque, a 14 tooth counter sprocket would be best.

text: I have owned many Bikes over the years the 650 v-strom is the best to date as an all rounder´ I have one big problem with it as Iam cruising along at around 100 KLM their is an enoying vibration in the handelbars (throttle)The Bike has done over 10,000 KLM, the service is due at 12,000 I have asked a few people about my problem & they say it could be that the throttle bodies are out of sinc & thats causing the vibes , I said that to the dealer & he swept it under the carpet but did say on the 12,000 KLM service the do check the throttle bodies then. The Bike shop Iam enoyed at because they did not check the throttle bodies at the 6,000KLM service when I mentioned the vibes I was having maybe its a big job ha, the bike is still under warranty, is their any one out their with the same problem??? Cheers Julian.
