author: Ian Scott
text: WEE STROM  .. USER TEST Rider :-  46 years , 5´ 9´´   ,    85 kg ....      17 years riding on different bikes. Wee strom mileage = 1500  miles Ok  so  its my turn to repay the debt .  Before buying my wee strom I read just about every review or test on the web ..  so here are my payback thoughts ---- I am not a Suzuki dealer, rep or bike journalist, just a plain old user living in Chile.….. First impressions are often correct  (said Talking Heads many moons ago ) and mine were and are still very very positive ..  this bike is fast, comfy (although the seat is only good for about an hour), and handles superbly. I love the light weight, good brakes, RESPONSIVE ENGINE and upright ergonomics.. This is a modern bike….  What I mean to say is that it makes my sporster appear like from the 15 century… .  everything is totally modern and up to date. This is to say everything works efficiently .. The 650 is an engine that likes to rev ….  If  your in love with the thump thump sound of a low reving bike then this is perhaps not the machine for you … personally  I  love the thump thump of british twins …  but for me the wee stroms  other gleaming characteristics make me forgive its racing bike sound which can be best described as muted in standard form .. so much so that the slight whine of the fuel pump can be heard whilst on the move ….. one of the notable aspects of the sound is that closing the throttle it sounds like a jet engine !   ….  You know when your coming into land and just before touchdown the pilot shuts back ….. its that whistly whine sound …   I am actually learning to like it !  being so quiet has its advantages of course,  I don’t annoy the neighbours late at night, and I can pull up in front of everyone sitting outside the pub and not create a scandal .. Its  a revy engine ….  Cruiser lovers  might begrudge the relatively high revs at cruising speed 5,000 = 70mph    6,000 = 80mph  indicated …. I often  feel for another top gear at these speeds on the motorway to bring the revs down !   and then I think “well it doesn’t matter because gas milage is good anyway ”  so a 6th gear indicator would be very useful...  that’s not to say I am a real plodder ….  your talking to a guy that took his sportster back into the shop because the engine was cutting out  ….   Only to be told by a startled dealer that the bike was fine ,,, it was just the rev limiter cutting  in all the time ! A downside to these quite high cruising revs is a buzzing in the handlebar …  after a 2 hr trip on a motorway last week, my right hand had “pins and needles” upon dismounting. This was partly cured by wearing a more padded glove in the palm area … I am thinking about putting tape or something around the handlebar mount to insulate this buzz … Ok now the screen ….  I actually like the screen !   after loads of naked bikes its my first time with a piece of plastic out front ….  And it helps …  its not perfect ….  But then again if you want to ride in a pocket of still air …  then buy an outright tourer …. Or maybe buy a larger aftermarket screen …  I have tried the 3 positions and the highest position I feel offers the least buffeting (and most protection). At this height I can just see above it to give a clear view of the road  although in urban surroundings the screen at this height would have to be clean because objects at less than 8 meters distance will be seen throught  it and not over it. I am contemplating taking it off altogether for city riding for this reason.  Interestingly if at speed I lean forwards to the point that my chest would be resting on a tank bag , then the buffeting ceases, but of course neck angle increases a little …. Perhaps Suzuki could perhaps move the screen back say six inches --- it would make all the difference, the screen feels just a little far forwards … Ergonomics and seat height I love the big chasis. On Chilean bad potholed roads a large bike offers more stability. It is a tall bike,  I can get on and off without problems whilist there is no bag strapped onto the rear seat … if there is then I need to find a kerb! Or develop a leg 90 degree cocked dismount (requires a little practice!) At a standstill both feet make ball contact. Handling Superb: I can point the bike at a bend and it holds the line no problem and is confidence inspiring … its easy to flick around with the wide bars .. the tyres are fine too … I have a couple of times locked the rear wheel on a panic stop situation in urban traffic .. nothing scary and was easily corrected. After the harley I am not used to having a responsive back brake ! In respect to the width of the bike,  … I have to think twice before paddling my way between two lines of cars at a traffic jam.  Will I get through ? on my old SRX600 the widest thing was my elbows, the wee strom has bars, and mirrors to calculate and the later tends to be at the exact same height as many 4X4 wing mirrors.. …  not a huge problem … I just have to think twice that’s all .. .. the suspension is light years better than my harley --- although experts have sounded its shortfalls …  I find it adequate … maybe a little rough but fine. Ground clearance is superb ..  no grounding of the pegs yet ….. the lights are great ,  the 190kg is fantastic, easy to roll in and out of the garage, off road ??? I agree with most testers,  its fine on most gravel and dry dirt roads ,,,  not so hot on deep mug or sand … Would I buy this bike again = yes definitely !! self canceling indicators would also be nice I got back on my sportster the other day and it was like getting into a 1920s ford T …  very nice to look at but …..

author: Patrick D Gaebel
text: I´ve had no issue with the EFI on my 2019 T100. I am to the point where I prefer EFI to carburation. It is also getting to the point, with the emissions restrictions, and with Triumph being a European company that carbs are on their way out.

author: Roy Theisen
text: Just purchase my 2014 T100 (Fusion White/Aurum Gold) second hand.  I don´t want to molest this girl with a load of aftermarket performance accessories, but I would like to make her more functional. Any sources to replace the bolts with SS to prevent rusting?  Any tricks to keep the chrome and casings their factory polished appearance? I´m looking for

author: Freyja Van Siren
text: Vintage Cafe Racer girl: The M15 Bonneville T100 Black is the first motorcycle I have ever received wrapped in plastic...they usually have a stack of parts missing, on order, or I am fabricating something. The

author: Garry sale
text: ride a T100 black 2014 daily all weather 60km round trip to work & weekend day rides back roads of north west Tasmania Australia. Fitted as tourer with summer screen, king & queen seat, high back rest, panniers not saddle bags & centre stand. Looking at attaching rear rack as well for looks & carrying that little bit extra two up. As is commonly said, it meets a relaxing style of riding. With the power in the right place, other than over 120 kph, illegal speed any way. My wife find it comfortable for day rides, no long tours yet & she has a total knee replacement which limits her somewhat. I get a lot of comments on looks, people not believing it´s a new bike as it definitely looks the part. I´ve had dukes, Hondas, BMWs,  Suzuki f750, including 2 DR @ a peagio large scooter. The triumph is my keeper it fits my riding style, reliable, looks, & I get to ride in period gear, waxed cotton etc shortie helmet & goggles. Definitely fun & a head turner

author: MC2
text: Reliving the dream of desire for the Triumph 6TA Thunderbird in 1963 in my late teenage life (had previously had the pleasure of owning a cool ´59

author: Gixxer75
text: Hi just bought a 2007 T100 used , mileage about 11000 km . How many liters of engine oil does the bike takes up with filter n without filter? And what kind of oil should I use?

author: RatFink09
text: I bought my T-100 new and now have over 30K miles without any serious problems, even after a low-speed crash and dealer repair. Did notice that without using a Battery Tender the original battery lasted only about 8 months (just changed out my second one recently). A minor glitch which seemingly has self corrected is that the tach needle would get somewhat squirrely at about 4K, the needle wavering +/- 500 rpm, but since replacing the battery that seems to have settled out. I don´t do much wrenching, other than oil changes, brake pads & chain adjustments. Would appreciate any comments/feedback from others.

author: Triumph in Key L
text: Hello. I just bought a 2008 Bonneville T100 in Arizona. (Claret and White)It will be delivered week after next. I  would appreciate any input regarding this bike. Thanks, Larry

author: Clayton Sanford
text: Recently purchased 2012 Bonneville T100.  Have put about 800mi on it so far.  Twice during operation the engine has died and when restarted the engine light comes on.  Then, after a while, the problem mysteriously corrects itself.  I am taking it back to the dealership as it is still under warranty but I don´t think they will disclose to me any chronic problems w/ Bonnevilles. Any Ideas? anyone?

author: Javier
text: Where are bonnieville built

author: IAB
text: Hi, I have owned T100 for almost a year, hereby wish to check with the group as to whether it is possible to change the rims and tyres of my T100 to the SE (which is a 17\

author: Tunza
text: Hey all, just bought a 2010 bonny..had a 78 model many moons ago.. Which was a piece of crap.. But I´m having another go.. Hoping these newer ninnies can cut the mustard.  Hello to you all

author: M.charlesworth
text: My bonneville T100now has thruxton guards frnt/rear ,straight through ( silencers ),120 jets ,no air induction ,19 tooth front ,43 tooth rear sprockets,no rear footpegs. weight savings :-1lb each footpegs ,sprockets 3lb,chainguard 2lb,silencers ( a lot )drive sprocket cover 4 lb. Now does 70mph@3.600 revs have hit 117mph .am still experimenting ,engine is 790cc standard spec .

author: Will Astill
text: Keep reading, the top bit´s just background but I do need help... I recently bought a nice shiny ´05 reg T100 Bonneville only to have issues with a damaged cam shaft. I got the cam repaired under warranty but the dealer isn´t keen on stripping the whole engine down to inspect the lower end (the cam damage looked like an oil delivery issue). I really like the bike and think the repairs will do the job but want to get some confidence back in the engine so I´ve decided to get some oil samples analysed for excessive wear so I can prove if the lower end is alright or not. This means I need a couple of comparator engines to take samples from and I was wondering if anyone would mind sending me three at various intervals as described below: 1. Following Oil/Filter change (i.e. new oil from the engine). 2. After 250 miles 3. After 500 miles The engine needs to be a carb model and have done less than 3.5k miles and I´ll need the samples between Sept 2010 and Nov 2010 to get the results back while the bike´s still under warranty. I´ll pay for your samples to be analysed and send you the results, I just need your oil. If you can help, please email me \

author: Heidar
text: I´m close to purchasing a 2004 Triumph T100 and would like to know any pros and cons from folks who own already. Thanks for your input

author: andy p
text: hi my rev counter stopped working can anyone help

author: Slim
text: Hi...I just purchased a used ´06 T100(2K Mls.). Out for a ride,went over R/R tracks,oil light came on,engine quit? Shut off,restarted,finished ride without any further issues.Engine oil level,on the full mark.Also curious as to what type&weight oil is best for this engine?   Thanks,Slim

author: Laura
text: I´m looking to have my T-100 painted and need the Triumph color codes for the tangerine/white paint.  Can anyone help me out?

text: who are the cheapest parts suppliers in australia or possibly us

author: John Edward Bolt
text: Where can you get a parts manual and supplier in Australia I live in south Australia and I have a 2006 T100 bonneville thanks in advance

author: mproveaux
text: BAD GEAR:  my 2006 t100 is in the shop.  I took it in three weeks ago after 2nd gear suddenly started grinding and making the loudest racket.  They finally got back with me and told me it was a bad gear and that it was a defect and not my fault, which I already assumed.  They told me that Triumph agreed to pay for the parts but not labor, which will cost me more than $700.  Anyone else have this problem?  My warranty recently ran out.  Any chance I can talk Triumph into covering the whole cost?  Any suggestions how to pursue this?  I will appreciate any feedback.  M

author: Dr. Mark
text: Anybody have experience with the non restrictive airbox and KN filters  that are sold for the T100? I have Predator pipes and have rejetted for them, what mods to the jets for both the pipes and airbox?

author: Mark Andersen
text: Love my Bonny. Just trying to get feedback from others. Need new silencers. Air box mods? Great Bike. Mine is titled 05 but is exactly the same as the tangerine/white 06.

author: vlad
text: whitch is better boneville or boneville t100 ?

author: Kramocker
text: Hi. Total newbie here. I´ve been riding my Bonnie T100 (Tangerine & White) for about eight months now...totally stock. Any suggestions for pipes? I hear it´s also a good idea to remove the air box an replace with \

author: Jack
text: I´ve got an 06 T100 Black and Red.  Overall a great ride.  My only gripe is the pipes are too quiet.  I have drilled out the baffles to 1/2\

author: Mark
text: I have a 2005 T100. The stock pipes had been replaced with D & Ds but it was too loud and annoying in pitch. Replaced those with Predator pipes ( black powder coated but chrome available) Sounds good (loud but not high pitched and irritating. picked up power too but had to rejet carbs. Pipes look great- a lot like the pipes on the Thruxton

author: Mark
text: I ride a T100 Tangerine and white 05. Replaced the pipes with Predator pipes. Looks much better (look like Thruxton pipes). Mine are black powder coated and so are my headers. You need to reject if you put these on. Improved power. Loud but not annoying in pitch like the D&Ds ( I DO NOT recommend them) I´m looking into nonrestrictive airbox too but have no experience there. Seems like a very good idea. Stock filters are very restrictive but I guess will need to rejet again to maximize power. Anyone out there that has made both pipe and airbox mods?

author: Johnny K.
text: I´d like to install a throttle lock for my ´06 T100 Bonnie. Has anyone attemted to install this type of device or otherwise offer any tips? Thanks.

author: Dionski
text: Hi folks! I´d like to get a Bonneville T100 2007, unfortunately there is no more 2007 already!! dealer propose me a new 2006 instead, for same price. My questions are; 1- Is there any difference between 2006 and 2007 model? 2- Did anybody know what´s coming up for 2008? (should I wait for a 2008)? Thanks a lot in advance for your appreciated comments!! I hope I´ll be part of the group of happy owner soon!!

author: Raptor
text: Well I don´t know about differences in Specs, but Dealer shall give you rabate or free accessories!

author: Jack
text: The 2006 and 2007 T100s are pretty much the same.  I have heard that the 08s will be fuel injected to comply with stricter emission standards.

author: bb900
text: will the thruxton mufflers fit the t100 or are there any megaphone style silencers availAble

author: Dr. Mark
text: NO. But Predator makes a set that looks like the Thruxtons and works great and sounds good too. Come in chrome and black powder coat. Need to rejet the carbs if you change pipes. My bike is Tangerine and white with a black,white orange corbin seat and black headers and predator pipes with the orange bug shield. Even the Harley guys like it.ps do not use the D&D pipes they look ok but the pitch is annoyingly high. The predators are loud but the tone is much mellower but they ar loud if you crank it.

author: Bill Dragoo
text: My fiance´ recently purchased a 2003 Bonneville, Centinial edition. Great bike but had really poor fuel mileage. We have "off-road" pipes, stock carbs with the emission tubes removed and airbox is opened up. I just re-jetted it a bit but haven´t ridden it enough to evaluate our mod´s. What´s the best setup for this bike? What kind of trick engine or chassis mods are being done? We´re new to the Triumph world. Shadetree

author: Bill D
text: Our ´04 Bonneville with off road pipes has idle trouble. Runs fine otherwise but dies if idled  under 1300 or so. Anyone else had this problem? Any suggestions? We have about 4,000 miles on the bike.

author: gwayne281
text: What comes standard with the T-100 that is not on the standard Bonnie and what is the price difference?

author: Bill Dragoo
text: I´d like to join the message group. Thanks, Bill

author: Elvis Ross
text: T 100 has Tachometer, Chrome engine and trans side covers and a two tone paint job with hand donr pin stripping or "coach lines" the difference is about $500. ER

author: dave lees
text: had similar problem on old enfield check for leaks between carbs and manifolds

author: Cuda_Hunter
text: Hoping to get some information about the ignition switch on my 2012 T100 I just purchased. The previous owner lost the ignition key, I am wanting to know if the are a  smart key setup with a chip or fob or just a plain key? The bike has been in storage for 4 years and previous could not tell me the answer. I have removed the key system and am going to bring it into the dealership, but would like to be more knowledgeable before asking them for help. Thanks in advance for any information on this subject that can be provided!

author: Bee
text: You want people to notice the bike your riding and give you a nod. Even Harley riders. Well thsi bike will make it happen. You want an easy fun ride? You want pride of ownership? This is the one. The fun does not wear off after 6 years, It gets better. Its a great Sunday crusier on those perfect riding days or not so perfect. Price right too.Steve McQueen would agree.

text: My bonneville T100now has thruxton guards frnt/rear ,straight through ( silencers ),120 jets ,no air induction ,19 tooth front ,43 tooth rear sprockets,no rear footpegs. weight savings :-1lb each footpegs ,sprockets 3lb,chainguard 2lb,silencers ( a lot )drive sprocket cover 4 lb. Now does 70mph@3.600 revs have hit 117mph .am still experimenting ,engine is 790cc standard spec .

author: Will Astill
text: Keep reading, the top bit´s just background but I do need help... I recently bought a nice shiny ´05 reg T100 Bonneville only to have issues with a damaged cam shaft. I got the cam repaired under warranty but the dealer isn´t keen on stripping the whole engine down to inspect the lower end (the cam damage looked like an oil delivery issue). I really like the bike and think the repairs will do the job but want to get some confidence back in the engine so I´ve decided to get some oil samples analysed for excessive wear so I can prove if the lower end is alright or not. This means I need a couple of comparator engines to take samples from and I was wondering if anyone would mind sending me three at various intervals as described below: 1. Following Oil/Filter change (i.e. new oil from the engine). 2. After 250 miles 3. After 500 miles The engine needs to be a carb model and have done less than 3.5k miles and I´ll need the samples between Sept 2010 and Nov 2010 to get the results back while the bike´s still under warranty. I´ll pay for your samples to be analysed and send you the results, I just need your oil. If you can help, please email me \

Any chance I can talk Triumph into covering the whole cost?  Any suggestions how to pursue this?  I will appreciate any feedback.  M

text: I´ve got an 06 T100 Black and Red.  Overall a great ride.  My only gripe is the pipes are too quiet.  I have drilled out the baffles to 1/2\

author: Mark
text: I have a 2005 T100. The stock pipes had been replaced with D & Ds but it was too loud and annoying in pitch. Replaced those with Predator pipes ( black powder coated but chrome available) Sounds good (loud but not high pitched and irritating. picked up power too but had to rejet carbs. Pipes look great- a lot like the pipes on the Thruxton

author: Mark
text: I ride a T100 Tangerine and white 05. Replaced the pipes with Predator pipes. Looks much better (look like Thruxton pipes). Mine are black powder coated and so are my headers. You need to reject if you put these on. Improved power. Loud but not annoying in pitch like the D&Ds ( I DO NOT recommend them) I´m looking into nonrestrictive airbox too but have no experience there. Seems like a very good idea. Stock filters are very restrictive but I guess will need to rejet again to maximize power. Anyone out there that has made both pipe and airbox mods?

author: Dr. Mark
text: NO. But Predator makes a set that looks like the Thruxtons and works great and sounds good too. Come in chrome and black powder coat. Need to rejet the carbs if you change pipes. My bike is Tangerine and white with a black,white orange corbin seat and black headers and predator pipes with the orange bug shield. Even the Harley guys like it.ps do not use the D&D pipes they look ok but the pitch is annoyingly high. The predators are loud but the tone is much mellower but they ar loud if you crank it.

author: R  Walton
text: I just got my 2017 T120 , 1200cc, Bonneville and being a seasoned Carb guy was shocked at the Ride by Wire throttle on the Triumph. It jerked and made power hard to control in the first 1 and 2 gears. I read where others hade the same problem. Well, once I learned to use the Rain Mode switch on the right handlebar it was much better to control. Also, it is something you have to get used to by not giving it a lot of gas all at once. I ordered the Booster Plug In module and will see how that works. But, for now, Rain Mode has solved the problem pretty will.

author: walter haynes
text: Bought a new 2007 speed triple in early 2008. has  aprox 3000 miles on it and have not really had any problems. temperatures have been high in alabama ~ 95 ´ and after pulling into garage and parking without indication of overheating after ~20 mile ride, i noticed a moderate amount of fluid on the floor which appeared to be antifreeze although it was more yellowish brown than blue. my expansion tank was empty. the fluid was exiting from a small hose at the base of the bike but without doing alot of disassembly i cannot find its origin. my service manuel does not show an overflow hose for the coolant system.any thoughts to cause and fix if needed? W.H.

author: Gordon
text: Be aware of the T300 series starter motor. Mine packed up in September 2008 taking with it the starter ring, which means removal of the motor. Antifreeze light on the dashboard has a tendency to glow, the dealer here told me the best thing to do was change the whole loom! which really isn´t an option. It was a short. Another short occurred in the headlamp blowing the 10amp fuse all the time, once again an electrician told me to upgrade to 15amp. On the other hand the motor is very strong and is almost service free. I´ve had my 95 speed triple since June 2007 and done 25-30000 miles since then. Hope this helps.

author: Brandon Lewis
text: my 95 tiger 900 is a BEAST! and the size in hight alone are just crazy lol but ive lowered the front an inch or 3 got my intake parts working great but ive had an issue with my wheels being to tight and brakes failing along with a new flat tire that i was not ready for.. anyway after removing rear wheel greasing up all the bits to re install out of excitment i didnt put enough time into gettingone of my rear nuts tight enough somehow and chain began to loosen and loosen and loosen (Idont know this mind you..) so as im riding some dude kinda speeds past obviously not knowing what hes up against so naturally with an almighty growl of the tiger (SCHOOOOOoomm) there i went fly past he feels dredful defeat turns off and im moving way to fast to pull into my destination. so i slowwww it on down go to loop around and as im doing so i just hit way to low of a speed and when this bike is going down ... its goin down hard n heavy. the bike falls as i jump off and rush back to flip the giant  and take off before cars could witness my stupidity.. as i start to let off the clutch in basically rpms ready for a drag run the chain just SMACK!! right into the surrounding parts and oil begins spewing everywhere along with coolant i believe. so... ive got the bike home thinking i just completely destroyed my Tiger and so far all ive found is to buy the 45 dollar sprocket cover that has the dipstick set up and im thinking that may fix the entire catastrophy. like it was just ready for that type of situation.. And THATS why i love my 95 Triumph 900!!

author: Tyler Zollinger
text: Hello, I have owned the 96 tiger for four years and put over 15,000 miles on it.  I would advise nothing but the Metzler Tourance tires for the bike.  I have ran on Brigestones and the Michelin T-66. The Tourance tires are more money but well worth it when you factor in the preformance and life you get out of the tire.  I rode on the rear for 8500 miles! I have never gotten that much mileage out of a dual sport tire. As for the forks... I thought the forks were to soft for me too so I explored my options.  The easiest solution was for me was to put shims under the fork caps and lower the forks in the clamps 3/4 of an inch.  This stiffened up the front suspension and helped the bikes handeling in the corners.  This solution provided good results and didnt cost anything!  You can use different fork oil also. I hope this helps, Good luck.

author: RM Jeffrey
text: Mate, Did you sort out your tyre problems?  I run Metzeler Tourance and have had a real good run and handling with them. Sometimes hard to get but worth it. bob

author: tash
text: metezler tourance tires,change the fork oil in the forks,from 10 weight to 15 weight,handles much better,I have had the same problem It feels like a different bike now with less rebound.

author: Doug
text: Hello I´m currently living in the Napa Valley Northern California an area with many great rides in almost any direction you wish to head off in,I bought myself a 99 Tiger 900 a few months and it has been a lot of fun though a little top heavy for a skinny guy like me but very ridable, until last Saturday where in the town of Sonoma I had just walked out of the bank pulled off my ski mask threw the large sack of cash into my ample rear storage compartment and tried to start my Tiger,I turned the key pulled in the clutch lever in and pressed the starter button, I heard a one moderately loud click from under the seat, my dash lights went out and there was no power for half an hour or so when I tried again and she stared right up.But after that the same click lights and power gone.I´m wondering if it could be the starter solinoid or maybe a relay ? my local Triumph dealer in Marin is moving their store and can´t see the bike for a week or so. Any advice or help with this little problem would be much appreciated and will help me take advantage of this lovely 75 degree weather we are having this week. Thanks Doug

author: Brandon Lewis
text: my 95 tiger 900 is a BEAST! and the size in hight alone are just crazy lol but ive lowered the front an inch or 3 got my intake parts working great but ive had an issue with my wheels being to tight and brakes failing along with a new flat tire that i was not ready for.. anyway after removing rear wheel greasing up all the bits to re install out of excitment i didnt put enough time into gettingone of my rear nuts tight enough somehow and chain began to loosen and loosen and loosen (Idont know this mind you..) so as im riding some dude kinda speeds past obviously not knowing what hes up against so naturally with an almighty growl of the tiger (SCHOOOOOoomm) there i went fly past he feels dredful defeat turns off and im moving way to fast to pull into my destination. so i slowwww it on down go to loop around and as im doing so i just hit way to low of a speed and when this bike is going down ... its goin down hard n heavy. the bike falls as i jump off and rush back to flip the giant  and take off before cars could witness my stupidity.. as i start to let off the clutch in basically rpms ready for a drag run the chain just SMACK!! right into the surrounding parts and oil begins spewing everywhere along with coolant i believe. so... ive got the bike home thinking i just completely destroyed my Tiger and so far all ive found is to buy the 45 dollar sprocket cover that has the dipstick set up and im thinking that may fix the entire catastrophy. like it was just ready for that type of situation.. And THATS why i love my 95 Triumph 900!!

text: I have had my Tiger (95) completely rebuilt just about new everything (apart from the carbs), But when you run it on the road once it is hot it misfires and there is no power at all. Lots of heads a scratching any thoughts please?

author: AT
text: Not perfectly familiar with  this bike by sounds like a coil pack or ignition sensors.  If using a coil pack once it gets hot it will allow current jump internaly.  Backfiring also? Would go that direction and make the timing/ spark advance appear to be off. Hope helps your direction anyway.

author: Ian
text: I guess the only thing we did not change was the coil pack, I was thinking the CDI unit might be faulty. The other thought was with new barrels and pistons she might need a colder plug, what do yuou think to that. Thanks for the advise

author: Jeremy
text: I bought the tiger about 6 months ago and it was not all together.  I put it back together the best I could, but I am having carb issues.  I have cleaned the carbs again and I am about to put back on the bike.  Do the two vent hoses attach anywhere or do they hang free?  I can´t find anywhere to attach them and the book I have is no help.  The carbs were off of the bike when I got it so I did not get to see where the vent hoses were.  If the bike does not work correctly this time I will be selling the parts on ebay.  Thanks for any help.

author: tash
text: The pipes connected to your carbs do not connect to anything,and are slotted down the either side of the frame,there is also a breather on the bottom of the the air box connecting to the engine.

This was followed by temporary misfire at 4500 revs but has now got worse so the bike runs like a pig and wont tick over. Changed the CDI unit for one bought on ebay - no difference. Checked for good spark on all three coils - ok... and changed the plugs - still no improvement. Any ideas anyone?

author: Mick
text: What tyres would you advise as front grip is poor on mine and do you think its worth changing the front springs to limit bounce back

author: Tyler Zollinger
text: Hello, I have owned the 96 tiger for four years and put over 15,000 miles on it.  I would advise nothing but the Metzler Tourance tires for the bike.  I have ran on Brigestones and the Michelin T-66. The Tourance tires are more money but well worth it when you factor in the preformance and life you get out of the tire.  I rode on the rear for 8500 miles! I have never gotten that much mileage out of a dual sport tire. As for the forks... I thought the forks were to soft for me too so I explored my options.  The easiest solution was for me was to put shims under the fork caps and lower the forks in the clamps 3/4 of an inch.  This stiffened up the front suspension and helped the bikes handeling in the corners.  This solution provided good results and didnt cost anything!  You can use different fork oil also. I hope this helps, Good luck.

author: RM Jeffrey
text: Mate, Did you sort out your tyre problems?  I run Metzeler Tourance and have had a real good run and handling with them. Sometimes hard to get but worth it. bob

author: tash
text: metezler tourance tires,change the fork oil in the forks,from 10 weight to 15 weight,handles much better,I have had the same problem It feels like a different bike now with less rebound.

author: Doug
text: Hello I´m currently living in the Napa Valley Northern California an area with many great rides in almost any direction you wish to head off in,I bought myself a 99 Tiger 900 a few months and it has been a lot of fun though a little top heavy for a skinny guy like me but very ridable, until last Saturday where in the town of Sonoma I had just walked out of the bank pulled off my ski mask threw the large sack of cash into my ample rear storage compartment and tried to start my Tiger,I turned the key pulled in the clutch lever in and pressed the starter button, I heard a one moderately loud click from under the seat, my dash lights went out and there was no power for half an hour or so when I tried again and she stared right up.But after that the same click lights and power gone.I´m wondering if it could be the starter solinoid or maybe a relay ? my local Triumph dealer in Marin is moving their store and can´t see the bike for a week or so. Any advice or help with this little problem would be much appreciated and will help me take advantage of this lovely 75 degree weather we are having this week. Thanks Doug

author: Dirk
text: I bought a 98 Tiger 6 months ago after having 3 different Africa Twins, I said I will never ride anything ells than a twin till a friend of mine gave me a ride on his Tiger, 2 weeks later I own it. It started battling to start, swinging till the battery wants do die and then it will start, but don’t let it die then or you’ll have to push. After warming up it will start easily. I replaced the battery with the hope but no luck. Send it for shimming today and hope that will fix it. Do you have any ideas for me; I really miss riding my scooter.

author: Danny Vitez
text: Hello to all of you! I´m presently looking for an anduro type bike. After trying out the BMW GS1200, the Suzuki Strom, Triumph tiger, aprilia ETV 1000 caponord,I´ve come to the debate between the triumph tiger 1998 and the aprilia ETV caponord. Of course the prises are not the same but the two bikes are very interesting.1998 tiger, 21000 km for less than 8500 cdn $ or the ETV 2003 ,800 km for 16600$ cdn. BUT someone told me that the triumph tiger 1995 to 1998 model where having transmition and-or engine problems! IS THIS TRUE? Also in canada the aprilia brand just got in therfore little if not no feedback can be found. ANY feedbacks or sugestions would be verry appreciated! Thank you for your time and for your help! danny

text: Hello I´m currently living in the Napa Valley Northern California an area with many great rides in almost any direction you wish to head off in,I bought myself a 99 Tiger 900 a few months and it has been a lot of fun though a little top heavy for a skinny guy like me but very ridable, until last Saturday where in the town of Sonoma I had just walked out of the bank pulled off my ski mask threw the large sack of cash into my ample rear storage compartment and tried to start my Tiger,I turned the key pulled in the clutch lever in and pressed the starter button, I heard a one moderately loud click from under the seat, my dash lights went out and there was no power for half an hour or so when I tried again and she stared right up.But after that the same click lights and power gone.I´m wondering if it could be the starter solinoid or maybe a relay ? my local Triumph dealer in Marin is moving their store and can´t see the bike for a week or so. Any advice or help with this little problem would be much appreciated and will help me take advantage of this lovely 75 degree weather we are having this week. Thanks Doug

author: Simon
text: Well I´ve have read various claims as to what sort of economy it might or might not get. Frankly I´m very happy with the economy in all situations. My best, and I dont care whether anyone beleives me or not is 4.44lts per 100klms when the bike had 11,100klms on the speedo from new, this is 63.6 mpg (UK Imperial galls) we have vast open roads here in Australia so it is easy to do this. But you have to ride the throttle carefully. The speed was between 100 and 110kph over a distance of 150klms on a still day, and the air temp. was 27C. with rider only and not carrying any gear. Riding in most temperatures and giving it the odd squirt mainly not exceeding 110kph (country riding) I usually always average 5.3lts per 100klm or 53.29 mpg (UK Imperial) rider only no gear. The worst Ive had,into a strong head wind and through twisting hilly roads was 6.7lts per 100klms or 42.16 mpg (UK Imperial)rider only, with top box and panniers full. I´ve kept an eye out over time more out of curiosity, these days I just fill er up as I know the econmoy will be related to how much throttle gets screwed on. Either way Im not complaining.

author: Thermos
text: Hi all, I’m experiencing an intermittent smart‑key detection failure on my 2025 Yamaha TMAX 560. The issue is unusual because it fails completely silently — no “Key Not Detected” message, no warnings, and no stored ECU errors. Symptoms • I can ride home normally in the evening, but the next morning the scooter is completely unresponsive it’s like no key is present. • The ignition occasionally refuses to activate with no dash warnings at all. • Emergency start works, and I’ve ridden up to 10 km to different locations, but the issue persists regardless of where I am. • I have two key fobs, both with new batteries, and the problem occurs with both. • After several days of only being able to emergency‑start, the key fob begins partially responding:• Seat button lights up and works • Ignition button lights up and clicks • But the bike still won’t start • Then, a day later, everything works normally again • This “gradual recovery” repeats each time the fault happens. • Dealer diagnostics show no fault codes. • The failure is random and disappears before the dealer can take the bike in (they have a backlog). • Emergency PIN start works every time. • Dealer cannot reproduce the issue.

author: maurimac
text: Hi I have just bought myself an almost new 2007 T-Max (2207 kms on clock) having owned a 2001 model for the past 6 years.  The old model was fine but at 15,000 miles it needed a chain tensioner replaced - no big deal.  Only other replacement was the battery after 7 years ! The 2007 model is quite an improvement - the fuel injection improves fuel consumption by 20% plus but the brakes with ABS are in a different league. The 2001 model was OK but did not inspire confidence whereas my 2007 is glued to the road with much lighter pressure on levers needed - it almost digs in. The standard screen on the 2007 caused much loud buffetting of my helmet - I changed it for the old T-max 500 one at some expense and quiet is restored. All in all, it is a great scooter - I use mine solely for leasure being retired for some years  and it proves the old saying \

author: chris wilsona
text: Recently bought 2014 Yamaha ttr125LE with a won´t start issue. Scott Spark It´s got fuel Fire and Air but it feels like there´s no compression in it I mean it is super easy to kick as if it had none. Did a compression test on it kicking it until it stopped which was only at about 30 PSI the wet compression test on it went up to 90. Unless I´m wrong I believe that means bad piston rings? Not sure I´m up for that one. I´m a decent mechanic I want to get them to tear down stuff I always get nervous. Was thinking of upgrading while I had it apart to the 150cc Big Bore. Any comments or advice on that? I´d like to get a little more power out of it but as I´ve never even ridden it not sure if it´s the best idea. Also. The carb on this thing is common 2 problems with the Jets I understand. And I´m not crazy about the duel throttle body. And the choke seems to have problems though I think I´ve straightened it out with a little PB Blaster penetrating oil. We´ll see if it works right when I get it back together. Hope I didn´t bite off more than I can chew and I hope this bikes worth it. Definitely got a sweet deal on it hopefully it won´t turn bitter. So finally I just like to ask for advice or recommendations on performance mods or any other tips that might be helpful. Like buying it from me. LOL kidding thanks for reading I look forward to seeing what you have to say.

author: dragon
text: rode this bike for 7+ hours and even though its 5 years old, its awesome. heavy trails in the Olympic mountains, (rainy season) we went through 60 foot puddles up to 3 feet deep, (yes, above the air intake) luckily i was able to lift the front up, and wheely through it, and just barely made it across, but all in all, this bike is extremely controllable (my 1st wheely btw). we rode till it was dark, and there was sloshy mudd, wet rocks, tree stumps, and trenches all over. the bike rode allday, and even though i stalled it,it started up every time. extremely reliable. i did buy new (larger) jets for the carb, (when i 1st bought it, it wouldn´t even start b/c the P.O. never drained the gas and left it for a year...(this clogs the jets...just buy large ones..(less than 5dollars). highly recomend this bike for beginners. i could´ve crashed, but the bike naturally wants to stand straight up, never deviates from my path (btw, always look where you WANT to go, and not at the rocks or pits...just look where you want to ride, and look ahead) have fun.

author: jackal
text: Top speed of the TT-R 230 is around 55-65 mph on dirt. I´ve gotten mine up to 78 mph on the highway but you have less traction in the dirt.

author: Elmol
text: How can I improve the performance (mainly raw power!) on my ´05 TTR 230 and spend as little as possible?

author: jackal
text: You can take the spark arrestor out for one thing I seen a little improvment when i did that and take out the hose ontop of the oil filter it just pops right out so it can breath better..you can take the screen out also on the oil filter box but i wouldnt recommend it. You can also pick up some Jets from the yamaha dealership and re-jet it to give it a little more boost. If you need any help with improving your bike my emails Drunkendragon@earthlink.net

author: charmon
text: u can buy a rev box for $60 which gives it more power. Or buy a fmf slip on for around $200 or take out the spark arrestor. BBR big bore kit for around $200. Or BBR high performance camshaft

author: Wogie
text: I just bought this bike and didn´t even notice that it didn´t have a kickstarter untill i got it home.Is there any way to add a kickstarter and how much am I looking at price wise?

author: jackal
text: As far as i know they do not make a kick starter conversion kit at the moment. According to my yamaha dealership here in Oregon They should be coming out with a kit in the next year or so.I´ve been wanting one to for my TT-R and i´ll let you know when they come out with one.

author: kevin
text: You can purchase a after market kick starter. Last time I looked at this it was valued at about $1,000 AUD !!! Keep the battery on a trickle charger. Once the battery is flat you can push it arround all day and it will not start. If you do not use the bike very often bleed the carbi bowl first to make sure the fuel level is OK, then use an external battery in conjunction with the existing battery to start. I have modified mt TTR by adding a external jump start peg (wire from positive battery terminal to a bolt secured through the plastic cover)this way when the battery is flat or I want to trickle charge it I do not need to remove the side covers). Other than this it is a great bike.

author: charmon3
text: there is no way to add a kickstarter to the bike, but the electric start is ten times easier. The battery very rarely dies.

author: Randy Goolsby
text: I´ve exceeded 70 mph with a smaller rear (45tooth)sprocket on several occasions but it´s really winding to do it and probably hard on the engine. *Sustained* high speed riding in my opinion would be best around 55 mph. This bike is light and really not intended for freeway cruising. Is your 150 Honda a scooter? If it is, you might find the TW is more versatile, especially if you want to get off the beaten path and do some trails once in a while. Hope this helps.

author: manbearing
text: nope my honda nsr 150 is a racing bike. btw wats the original spocket size for the rear?

author: Randy Goolsby
text: Original sprocket size is a 50 tooth. It´s possible that a 40 or even a 35 might work but I was concerned with taking too much from the trail performance and off-road gearing. The 45 tooth boosted my cruising speed from 45-50 to 50-55 without winding the engine too much. If all I wever wanted from the bike was road manners, I´d have gone with the 40. FYI,(and anyone reading this that owns a TW)when I replaced the tires last month, I thought I was ordering identical replacement OEM knobbies when I ordered the Bridgestone TW 204s. They´re not knobbies. They pretty closely resemble the street tires that BMW puts on their 650 Dakar and Suzuki uses on their 600 or 650 dual-sport bikes. The tread´s deep but they look like street tires. They work good on hard-packed dirt but absolutely not good in mud or especially sand. On the road they shine. Very quiet and softer rubber compound makes for good adhesion. On a wet road (like any tires on a bike) they can be squirrely. Obviously there are going to be trade-offs with a dual-sport bike of ANY kind; this is the nature of dual-sport. These tires compromise off-road performance but benefit street riding. The ideal solution would be to have a spare set of OEM knobbies mounted on wheels with hubs. When you´re going to be doing exclusively street riding, mount the street tires and wheels (with smaller rear sprocket) on it and swap back for trail and inclimate weather. Just a suggestion. Randy

author: Randy Goolsby
text: You´re welcome! I hope you get a TW; you won´t be disappointed. If or when you get it, post here and I´ll get a notice. I want to know what you ended up buying, what you paid for it and all the other details. Are you in the U.S. or another country? Here in the states, winter´s coming fast and there are good deals to be had on just about any motorcycle you can name. I watch E-Bay and you´ll find TWs there all the time that are anywhere from 1-10 years old with VERY low miles on them and roughly 1/2 to 2/3 the price of a new one. People buy them and then don´t use them much. I bought my 2000 in April this year. It had just over 560 miles on it and I paid under $2100 for it. New ones were going for $3600. Since I bought it, I have put over 4000 miles on it and the only problems I had were carburetor related and the dealer took care of it for about $50. I posted a picture of it earlier today on this website. Hopefully they´ll post it under TW 125. Good Luck! Randy

author: snorby
text: 2015 Yamaha XT250 is blowing the ignition fuse as soon as the bike is turned on. The battery was nearly dead and needed to be jumped to get started.  I rode about thirty feet and it died - as if it was turned off and just lugged to a stop.  Tried jumping it again and it did nothing. So I replaced the battery with a fully charged one. When turning the key on, it doesn´t make any of its normal start up sounds, the dash doesn´t come to life, the horn doesn´t work, and of course it doesn´t start.  However, the running lights (front and back) work, including turn signals and brake light.  Did some checking on the fuses to find that the ignition fuse was blown but all other fuses were fine, including the 30a fuses by the starter relay.  I replaced the 10a ignition fuse and tried again, only to have the fuse blow again instantaneously when turning it on. Any idea where the problem could be?  Also, are there more fuses other than the two packs next to the battery and the ones next to the starter relay?

author: Ron
text: I purchased this bike used recently. The sprockets and chain were bad so I had them replaced. It had a 15/48 and 428 chain. I put a 15/51 set on and didn´t like it. So I bought a 45T rear and it is better. After reading the mnl it stated the following transmission ratio: 1st/2.846, 2nd/1.813, 3rd/1.318, 4th/1.036, and 5th/0.821.  With the 15/45 sprockets now I get a final ratio of 3.0. My question is if I went to a 16/48 sprockets (still a 3.0 final ratio) what kind of performance can I expect.  Thanks for a reply.

author: Steelboy32
text: I purchased this bike as a barely running, missing many pieces about 10 years ago in Bloomfield PA.  Since then I have had the frame straightened, added multiple parts and just recently had the top end done. I have an 1990 XT 350 also.  I went through this process and the 350 starts 1st kick and runs like a champ, every time. The 250 is just a mother to start!  I just replaced the coil and igniter box under the tank.  I have a very strong spark.  It takes 30-40 kicks where it doesn´t even sputter, then it fires right up out of no where and once warmed up runs very well.  Me, and my mechanic are baffled?  I thought it was electrical but now It seems carburetor?  My next move is pull the carb, completely clean it and check the float, float needle and main jet for blockage. Also synchronization of the two carb pieces. Please advise, thank you, Steelboy32

author: Dave
text: Bought the yz250x new as was the lightest 250 2 strke bike at 104kg fuelled ready to ride. I did have to rejet carb as it killed 3 plugs within 1 hour of riding  due to the factory jetting. Standard Jetting - Main jet #180, pilot jet #52, Jet neddle clip N3EW-3 @ 30:1 fuel ratio Rejetted - Main jet #178, pilot jet #45, Jet needle clip N3EW-2 @ 50:1 fuel ratio Bike has done almost 50hrs now and have not touched it other than oil and air filter changes. Only mentioned the above jetting to save other riders the drama of trial and error in getting it right. (Take note yamaha NZ) Would buy again as a great bike.

author: Dave
text: Bought the yz250x new as was the lightest 250 2 strke bike at 104kg fuelled ready to ride. I did have to rejet carb as it killed 3 plugs within 1 hour of riding  due to the factory jetting. Standard Jetting - Main jet #180, pilot jet #52, Jet neddle clip N3EW-3 @ 30:1 fuel ratio Rejetted - Main jet #178, pilot jet #45, Jet needle clip N3EW-2 @ 50:1 fuel ratio Bike has done almost 50hrs now and have not touched it other than oil and air filter changes. Only mentioned the above jetting to save other riders the drama of trial and error in getting it right. (Take note yamaha NZ) Would buy again as a great bike.

author: Dave
text: Bought the yz250x new as was the lightest 250 2 strke bike at 104kg fuelled ready to ride. I did have to rejet carb as it killed 3 plugs within 1 hour of riding  due to the factory jetting. Standard Jetting - Main jet #180, pilot jet #52, Jet neddle clip N3EW-3 @ 30:1 fuel ratio Rejetted - Main jet #178, pilot jet #45, Jet needle clip N3EW-2 @ 50:1 fuel ratio Bike has done almost 50hrs now and have not touched it other than oil and air filter changes. Only mentioned the above jetting to save other riders the drama of trial and error in getting it right. (Take note yamaha NZ) Would buy again as a great bike.

author: Runningman
text: A couple of weeks ago I sold my 2001 Hayabusa, and bought a 2000 R1 hoping to have fun in the twisties again, but suffered a rude awakening. Earlier this week, I had a nasty tankslapper going around a choppy corner on my ´00 R1, exiting at about 120-130km my front end skipped and jumped causing the above incident. The bike and I survived a violent situation, and I am now wondering if I should get a steering damper? Which brand and style is best for this bike, or should I just slow down. I expect with the damper, I will push it harder and not survive the next one. Any input would be appreciated. Already contemplating a Goldwing or something more sedate as an alternative.  For the record, the R1 does roll into corners quicker and easier, but the Busa did have stability, comfort, and smoothness, wrapped up in an unfortuantly bulbous looking package.  Had it the lines of the R1 and the handling, it may have been the ultimate sport bike.  Anyway, what steering damper do I need here?

author: Runningman
text: A couple of weeks ago I sold my 2001 Hayabusa, and bought a 2000 R1 hoping to have fun in the twisties again, but suffered a rude awakening. Earlier this week, I had a nasty tankslapper going around a choppy corner on my ´00 R1, exiting at about 120-130km my front end skipped and jumped causing the above incident. The bike and I survived a violent situation, and I am now wondering if I should get a steering damper? Which brand and style is best for this bike, or should I just slow down. I expect with the damper, I will push it harder and not survive the next one. Any input would be appreciated. Already contemplating a Goldwing or something more sedate as an alternative.  For the record, the R1 does roll into corners quicker and easier, but the Busa did have stability, comfort, and smoothness, wrapped up in an unfortuantly bulbous looking package.  Had it the lines of the R1 and the handling, it may have been the ultimate sport bike.  Anyway, what steering damper do I need here?
